responseSet.title: Rapaport, Rangell, and General Theory
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Rapaport, Rangell, and General Theory

Joseph Lifschutz

Joseph Lifschutz

5825 Nottingham Drive
El Sobrante, CA 94803
Fax: 510-222-2623

Joseph Lifschutz  

November 26, 2003. I wish to note an unfortunate omission in Howard, Shevrin's“The Consequences of Abandoning a Comprehensive Psychoanalytic Theory: Revisiting Rapaport's Systematizing Attempt” (JAPA51/3). Shevrin explains how psychoanalysis is foundering because of the long-standing lack of recognition of the need for a general, comprehensive theory on a higher conceptual level than clinical theory. Shevrin refers to the overarching theory we once had, now supposedly outdated—David Rapaport's metapsychology. Shevrin's article is an eloquent call for a return to our conceptual roots.

But missing is any reference to the lifelong contributions of Leo Rangell. For decades he has never stopped advocating, as in his recent “The Theory of Psychoanalysis: Vicissitudes of Its Evolution” (JAPA50/4), a “single unif ied theory of psychoanalysis,” conceived of as a “superordinate theory” embracing Freud's “dynamic, genetic, topographic, economic, and structural viewpoints,” as well as “the adaptive viewpoint… coming basically from the formulations of Hartmann and Rapaport…. Any psychological investigation would do well to employ all six metapsychological points of view” (pp. 1109, 1112 - 1113). Compare with Shevrin: “Rapaport referred to the theory he was sketching as ‘general’ in order to distinguish this superordinate level of theory from what he called ‘special clinical theory,’ whose principles were to be derivable from the general theory…. It was Rapaport's intention… not to abandon clinical theory but to ground it in more fundamental principles” (pp. 1006-1007). Obviously these two thinkers see eye to eye in this very important area. Indeed, Rangell made a point of crediting Shevrin with adhering to Rapaport's view while most of his colleagues who were coworkers with Rapaport, including George Klein, Merton Gill, Robert Holt, and Roy Schafer, abandoned Freud's metapsychology (Rangell, p. 1119). One consequence of this, Shevrin points out, is that “it brought to a halt the beginnings of a science based on cumulative knowledge” (p. 1006). Shevrin fails to note the one exception: that Rangell has been steadily developing just such a cumulative theory for the last half-century. It is unfortunate that Rangell does not receive equivalent recognition from Shevrin.

[[RunningHead]]

Howard Shevrin Replies

Howard Shevrin

Howard Shevrin

2021 Vinewood Boulevard
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
Fax: 734-764-4031
E-mail: shevrin@umich.edu

Howard Shevrin  

December 23, 2003. Joseph Lifschutz is absolutely right. It was an unfortunate error on my part not to mention Leo Rangell's important contributions over many years to the very issues addressed in my article. In fact, not long after my article appeared I received an e-mail from Rangell calling the oversight to my attention. I responded with an apology and a desire to rectify the situation. Lifschutz's letter gives me that opportunity. If only we had more Leo Rangells!

responseSet.title: Letter: Confoundng Factors in Research Populations

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter

Confoundng Factors in Research Populations

Jonathan Rosenfeld

Jonathan Rosenfeld

425 West 59th Street, Suite 4D
New York, NY 10019
E-mail: jer3@columbia.edu

Jonathan Rosenfeld  

July 24, 2004. I would like to congratulate the editors, particularly Stuart Hauser, for the outstanding section on research in JAPA 52/2. Sidney Blatt and Golan Shahar's “Psychoanalysis—with Whom, for What, and How?” is an impressive contribution to that section. I greatly appreciated the authors' careful explanation of their statistical results, an exercise that rendered the numbers they presented intelligible to the general reader.

I would like to encourage both the editors and the authors to take their presentation a step further. In a study that is not randomized, it is possible that various factors may be distributed unequally among the groups being compared. Blatt and Shahar provide an example of a possible confounding factor in their Figure 10. That figure shows that patients in the supportive-expressive psychotherapy group had a lower number of Rorschach responses than patients in the psychoanalysis group. Any difference between the treatments may be explained, at least in part, by the difference in the patient populations before treatment. The Menninger Psychotherapy Research Project began with a two-week evaluation. The MPRP data on these patients included education level, socioeconomic class, presence or absence of substance disorder, and IQ. In the APA Grand Rounds Online (2/19/2004)—“From Science to Practice: How to Critically Evaluate the Literature”—S. Nassir Ghaemi states that “it is the responsibility of investigators to think about and examine such potential confounders.… The first table should generally be a comparison of clinical and demographic variables in the groups being studied to see if there are any differences, which then might be confounders” (www.psych.org/members/grandrounds/7evaluate/archive.cfm). It would refine and deepen your presentation to include a discussion of these possible confounding factors, as well as any others the authors may have information about.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Neuroscience

Brain Gender. By Melissa Hines. New York: Oxford University Press, 2004, 307 pp., $49.50.

James Leckman

James Leckman

230 South Frontage Road
New Haven, CT 06520
E-mail: james.leckman@yale.ed

James Leckman   Neison Harris Professor of Child Psychiatry, Pediatrics, and Psychiatry and Director of Research, Child Study Center, Yale University.

Melissa Hines is a professor of psychology and director of the Behavioural Neuroendocrinology Research Unit at the City University of London. She displays an impressive expertise in the biological and behavioral determinants of sex differences. This reflects her training both as a neuroscientist and as a clinical psychologist.

From the outset she reminds us that men are more likely than women to be incarcerated for violent crimes, to become university professors, to hold positions of political power, and to have above-average incomes.

[[RunningHead]]

Abstracts of the 2005 Poster Session of the American Psychoanalytic Association Winter Meeting

Depression: A Case of Misdirected Aggression?

Suzanne Haddad , Jenae Neiderhiser , Erica Spotts and David Reiss

David Reiss

Warwick 3rd Floor
2300 K Street, NW
Washington, DC 20037
E-mail: SuzanneKerin@yahoo.com

David Reiss  

This study investigated Freud's theory of depression as aggression directed toward the self (1930). While considered by many to be a basic tenet of psychoanalytic theory, the relationship between internally directed aggression and depressive symptoms lacks strong empirical validation and runs the risk of being overlooked due to economic and political factors. Using existing data from the Twin and Offspring Study in Sweden (TOSS), we examined the relationship, in adults, between depressive symptoms and different forms of aggression.

Method

The TOSS sample was drawn from the Swedish Twin Registry (Lichtenstein et al. 2003) and consists of 544 pairs of adult twin women, their male partners, and adolescent children (one per couple) and 317 pairs of adult twin men, their spouses, and adolescent children (one per couple), resulting in a total sample of 861 pairs of twins and their families (5,166 individuals). Assessment included a wide range of relationship, personality, and mental health questionnaires. For the purposes of this study, we examined the responses of the adult twins’ (1,722 individuals) to two of the questionnaires administered; the Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression Scale (Radloff 1977), measuring depressive symptoms and the Karolinska Scales of Personality (Schalling and Edman 1993), measuring externally and internally directed aggression.

1291

[[RunningHead]]

Abstracts of the 2005 Poster Session of the American Psychoanalytic Association Winter Meeting

Making Friends Through Warmth and Charm: Developing and Applying a new Measure of Social Competence

Justine Larson and Sarah Whitton

Sarah Whitton

1011 Olive Street
Baltimore, MD 21230
E-mail: jlarson8@jhmi.edu

Judge Baker Children's Center
53 Park Hill Avenue
Boston, MA 02120
E-mail: swhitton@jbcc.harvard.edu

Sarah Whitton  

This poster presents the background and process involved in developing a new measure of peer-reported young adult social competence using Q-sort methodology. Although social competence has been defined variously in the literature, it is defined here as the attainment of developmentally appropriate goals in social contexts (Ford 1982). In longitudinal and cross-sectional research, social competence has been shown to be associated with a spectrum of positive outcomes, including lower levels of psychopathology, enhanced coping, future social adjustment, and lower delinquency and academic dropout rates (Parker and Asher 1987). The ability to recruit and maintain friendships is a key aspect of positive psychosocial functioning, across the life span and for young adults specifically (Rubin, Bukowski, and Parker 1998).

Method

The measure of peer-reported young adult social competence that we created is a Q-sort “mega-item,” developed using Q-sort methodology (Block 1974). A Q-sort is an arranged set of descriptive items. Friends, nominated by the subject being studied, distribute items into nine piles, from “least characteristic” to “most characteristic” of the subject, and this distribution of items represents the friends’ perceptions—a “map” of the Q-sort items—of a person they know well. One application of the Q-sort is a mega-item, a selection (usually 5-15 items) of Q-sort items that are aspects of a particular characteristic. The placement of each item in the Q-sort is treated as a score (1 - 9). Each component's score is then added to obtain a “mega-item” score, which is the score for that particular characteristic.

The measure was developed and tested using data from Hauser and colleagues’ 25-year longitudinal research project (Hauser 1991), using peer-rated Q-sorts of 133 young adults ages 23 to 25. The subjects had originally been recruited at ages 13 to 15 from two groups, a psychiatrically hospitalized nonpsychotic group and a high school control group. As young adults, the subjects selected two peers who knew them well.

[[RunningHead]]

Abstracts of the 2005 Poster Session of the American Psychoanalytic Association Winter Meeting

Approaches to Psychotherapy of Trauma: Differences Among Psychoanalysts, Psychodynamic Clinicians, and Cognitive Therapists

Michele Schottenbauer , Diane Arnkoff , Carol Glass and Sheila Gray

Sheila Gray

Department of Psychiatry Massachusetts General Hospital
15 Parkman Street WAC 812
Boston, MA 02114
E-mail: mschottenbauer@partners.org

Sheila Gray  

Posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) is a response to extreme psychological stress that affects about 8% of the general population in their lifetime (American Psychiatric Association 2000). Randomized clinical

1315

trials (RCTs) have demonstrated the effectiveness of cognitive-behavior therapy (CBT) and Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing, or EMDR (Chambless and Ollendick 2001). However, only one RCT (Brom, Kleber, and Defares 1989) has included psychodynamic treatment for PTSD (Roth and Fonagy 1996), despite research showing that more primary care patients report receiving psychodynamic interventions (30%) than cognitive-behavioral interventions (26%) or exposure (16%) for PTSD (Rodriguez et al. 2003).

Research suggests that clinicians’ theoretical orientations may not correspond in a predicable or obvious way with their interventions (Beutler, Machado, and Neufeldt 1994). When treating a PTSD patient, clinicians may alter their customary technique to address issues specific to PTSD (Herman 1997). To investigate this phenomenon, psychoanalysts, psychodynamic clinicians, and cognitive therapists were invited to participate in an online survey of therapeutic approaches to patients with PTSD in an effort to discover commonalties between, and defining characteristics within, each group of respondents.

Method

Participants. Therapists identifying themselves primarily with psychodynamic/psychoanalytic and cognitive-behavioral practice were recruited from major professional organizations via announcements disseminated by e-mail. Those who expressed interest were directed to an internet-based survey approved by the CUA Human Subjects’ Committee.

Demographic/educational history questionnaire. The demographic questionnaire included questions about therapist background, such as highest professional degree and core professional identif ication. Participants were asked to select the approach that best represented their treatment of clients/patients and to rate their overall adherence to specif ic subtheories and approaches on a scale from 1 (not at all) to 7 (a great deal).

responseSet.title: Abstracts of the 2005 Poster Session of the American Psychoanalytic Association Winter Meeting: Individual Personality Characteristics and Their Link to Couples’ Relationship Satisfaction
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Abstracts of the 2005 Poster Session of the American Psychoanalytic Association Winter Meeting

Individual Personality Characteristics and Their Link to Couples’ Relationship Satisfaction

Darshana Lele and Morton Kissen

Morton Kissen

389 East 89th Street, Apt. 5J
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: darshl78@yahoo.com

Morton Kissen  

People bring their own personal characteristics into relationships. Mate selection based on a partner's personality has been of particular interest and has led to conflicting psychological theories. In social psychology this is called the similarity-attraction effect (Newcomb 1956). This perspective suggests that individuals choose romantic partners with characteristics that are socially valued. For example, values like female beauty are important to males, while women tend to value social status in males. But in psychodynamic literature the focus is on how one's individual personality influences the choice of a partner with dissimilar personality traits. This is known as the complementarity perspective and relates closely to the phenomenon of “opposites attract.” Some support has been demonstrated for this concept in biology and sadomasochisitic relationships (Baumeister 1986; Buss 1985). A contrasting perspective on partner choice is that of similarity, which postulates that we are attracted to people with personality characteristics like our own (“Like begets like”). The aims of the current study were to test which of those two perspectives holds more true in partner choice, and how dyadic personality meshes are correlated with relationship satisfaction. We added relationship satisfaction as a variable, to see whether there were any gender differences in satisfaction when a partner had similar or dissimilar personality traits.

Method

A heterogeneous sample of 28 couples currently in a romantic relationship participated in this study. The couples were undergraduate Adelphi University students and/or friends and family members.

[[RunningHead]]

Abstracts of the 2005 Poster Session of the American Psychoanalytic Association Winter Meeting

The Relation of Shame to Anger in Intimate Adult Relationships

Kristina Harter , Robert Waldinger and Marc Schulz

Marc Schulz

1249 Boylston Street
Boston, MA 02155
E-mail: kharter@partners.org

Marc Schulz  

This study examined the relation between shame and anger in couples who are at risk for relationship dissolution. The purpose of our research is to identify underlying processes that may impact adults’ capacities to regulate their emotions in intimate relationships. Unlike previous research on the relation of shame to anger, the current study distinguished shame from guilt and used a multidimensional assessment of anger. To date, research on the relation of shame to anger has been hindered by disagreement over the moral, self-regulatory functions of shame.

Methods and Hypotheses

The participants were 100 couples recruited from the Boston metropolitan area for a study of couples at particular risk for relationship dissolution. This high-risk sample was gathered by focusing recruitment efforts on women who had histories of childhood sexual abuse (50 couples) and couples with recent histories of physical violence (50 couples). Eligible couples had to be married or living together for a minimum of 12 months prior to participating in the study. The mean age for men was 32.1 years (SD = 9.0) and the mean age for women was 31.3 years (SD = 9.3). Thirty-five couples (35%) were married and 65 (65%) were living together in a committed relationship for a minimum of one year.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and (Auto)Biography

My Life in Theory. By Leo Rangell. A JAPA Book. Edited by Fred Busch. New York: Other Press, 2004, 363 pp., $35.00.

Jerome Winer

Jerome Winer

122 South Michigan Avenue
Chicago, IL 60603
E-mail: jwiner@psych.uic.edu

Jerome Winer   Training and Supervising Analyst, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis; Professor of Psychiatry, University of Illinois at Chicago.

Leo Rangell has spent six decades contributing to scientif ic theory, five of them to psychoanalysis. He has served as president of both the American Psychoanalytic Association and the International

1371

Psychoanalytical Association, each of them twice. Rangell has written 450 articles and seven books, almost all of them still of more than historical interest. He has remained a clear-thinking, cogent writer dealing with salient theoretical issues well into his nineties. His recent e-mails on the members’ open-line of the American Psychoanalytic Association are but one proof of this fact.

Rangell has personal disdain for the term “classical analysis” because of its connotation of crumbling Greek or Roman columns. He himself has stood solidly in place demonstrating the value and currency of a good deal of Freud's discoveries and their theoretical explication, as well as the value of contributions made by those who followed him without major deviation. Rangell never claimed Freud “said it all” but went on to demonstrate how Freud's basic canon could be expanded and classified. This book documents these efforts and does so in a readable, informative fashion.

Rangell has read everything of importance to psychoanalysis, whether he agrees with it or not. He has carried on active correspondence with many younger and aspiring psychoanalytic writers, providing them generously with clear commentary, always with the aim of strengthening and facilitating their ef forts. But is all his ef fort to maintain a unitary “mainstream” psychoanalytic theory similar to the often brilliant efforts of those disclaiming any biological role in the etiology of schizophrenia and insisting on a unitary psychological cause? I would say no. To me, Rangell's Life in Theory is an up-to-date book well written.

If there is a bête noir in psychoanalytic theorizing to Rangell, it is the centrifugal and remodeling force leading to fragmentation of the unitary theory that he considers truly psychoanalytic. Rangell is against the pluralism endorsed by Wallerstein or even simpler divisions such as George Klein's separation of metapsychology and clinical theory. The very ground held to be common by diverging schools is to Rangell not really common at all. Even such distinctions as the past and present unconscious of Joseph and Anne-Marie Sandler are reduced by Rangell to the unconscious and preconscious of Freud. None of the alternative psychoanalytic theories are wrong, Rangell claims, only incomplete. Emphasis on only one part creates a dog without a tail or limbs, or even worse, turns the tail into the dog.

Richard Vann

Richard Vann

100 Kensington Church Street
London, W8 4BU, England
E-mail: rvann@wesleyan.edu

Richard Vann   Professor of History and Letters, emeritus, Wesleyan University; Senior Editor, History and Theory; President, International Commission on the History and Theory of Historiography.

Methodism and psychoanalysis—together at last! And, with a born-again Methodist guiding the affairs of the United States, it's about time

In this intriguing book, Keith Haartman offers us a psychoanalytic interpretation of the only religious movement in English history that ever managed to become something like a mass movement. On the cover is a picture of John Wesley preaching while standing on the grave of his father,1 which seems to foreshadow the text to follow. It turns out, however, to be refreshingly free of finding oedipal symptoms everywhere. Haartman's approach is sensitive to the nuances of the lives and thoughts he is analyzing, and makes an heroic though perhaps not totally successful effort not to pathologize religious experience.

His argument is set forth in the first three chapters, especially the third; the remaining six enrich it.

1Like all surviving pictures of Wesley, this is an idealization; we have no real portrait or likeness.1375

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Developmental Theory

The Internal World and Attachment. By Geoff Goodman. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 2002, 339 pp., $57.50.

Richard Honig

Richard Honig

3001 Highland Avenue
Cincinnati, OH 45219
Fax: 513-961-1530
E-mail: rhonig@3001.us

Richard Honig   Training and Supervising Analyst, Cincinnati Psychoanalytic Institute.

“Even though I walk through the darkest valley, I will fear no evil, for you are with me.… Therefore my heart is glad and my tongue rejoices; my body will also rest secure, because you will not abandon me to the grave.… You will fill me with joy in your presence” (Psalms 23: 4; 16: 9-11; cited on p. 25).

With biblical passages such as this, Geoff Goodman introduces most chapters of The Internal World and Attachment. In so doing he not only lyrically describes the basic and universal sentiments of human attachment and their primacy to human nature and development, but also, perhaps, hints at the magnitude of the task he has set for himself in his attempt to integrate two major strands of contemporary psychoanalytic theory.

It is of considerable interest that Goodman's 2002 volume follows so closely on the heels of Peter Fonagy's 2001 Attachment Theory and Psychoanalysis. These two works, taken together, constitute a major contribution to putting to rest the remnants of the straw dogs of differences through which earlier adherents of attachment theory and psychoanalytic theory have made caricatures of each other and lay the necessary foundation for the further genuine integration of the enormous contributions attachment theory offers to psychoanalysis.

Fonagy begins his work with the statement, “There is bad blood between psychoanalysis and attachment theory. As with many family feuds, it is hard to identify where the problem began” (p. 1). He takes the position that the relationship between attachment theory and psychoanalysis is more complex than adherents of either community generally recognize and that modern psychoanalysis does not overlook the child's need for a relationship.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Abstracts of the 2005 Poster Session of the American Psychoanalytic Association Winter Meeting

On Potential Space in Psychosomatics and Psychosis: A Rorschach Study

Shira Tibon

Shira Tibon

12 Lea Street
Tel-Aviv 69412
ISRAEL
E-mail: stibon@mta.ac.il

Shira Tibon  

The present study applies Winnicott's conceptualization of potential space (1971) to explore the similarity between psychosomatics and psychosis, through using a new Rorschach index, the Reality-Fantasy Scale, or RFS (Tibon, Handelzalts, and Weinberger 2005). Following Smith (1990), who interprets the Rorschach in terms of Ogden's model (1985) of different forms of collapse of potential space, the RFS operationalizes this model by using variables derived from the Comprehensive System, or CS (Exner 2003), the most widely used method for scoring and interpreting the Rorschach.

Method

Participants. In Study A (N = 76), participants included 41 adult nonpatients and 35 adult psychiatric patients with a referral question concerning psychosis. In Study B (N = 92), participants were Italian adult medical patients with Irritable Bowel Disease, IBD, usually con sidered a psychosomatic disease, who were included in a Rorschach study of alexithymia (Porcelli and Meyer 2002).

Measures. The Reality-Fantasy Scale (RFS) consists of a group of CS variables used to assess the input of reality and fantasy in the Rorschach response (Tibon, Handelzalts, and Weinberger 2005). The CS variables were included in the RFS following a theory-based selection and a comprehensive item-level analysis. The 11-point scale ranges from -5 (reality collapse into fantasy) to +5 (fantasy collapse into reality). An RFS score of -5 represents extreme reliance on fantasy and is given to responses with minimum contact with external reality (e.g., It is a bat. Oh … it is approaching toward me). An RFS score of +5, on the other hand, represents extreme reliance on reality and is given to responses with minimal input of fantasy (e.g., This looks like a cloud because of its shape). The RFS scoring of each response of a Rorschach protocol and the RFS mean and standard deviation of the protocol (RFSP and RFS-S respectively) are calculated by the RFS software (Tibon and Suchowski 2005).

The Ego Impairment Index, EII-2 (Viglione, Perry, and Meyer 2003), is a CS measure of psychological impairment and thought disorder. The current version of the index comprises five subcomponent variables, entered with different weights to an equation.

[[RunningHead]]

Comings and Goings

Steven Levy

Steven Levy

Steven Levy  

Having completed my first year as editor of JAPA, I am more appreciative than ever of the people of the journal. Their hard work, imagination, and teamwork make turning out JAPA both a privilege and a pleasure.

We have moved the JAPA office to Atlanta and have gone paperless (almost). Thanks to the prodigious talents of Lynn Cullen, our editorial assistant, this has all seemed (to the outside) effortless. Her warmth and grace facilitate our editorial work in ways the board and I observe with wonder. Our first-ever managing editor, Marilyn Herleth, oversees JAPA operations with firm control, infectious humor, and New York grit. I have been lucky enough to have persuaded the former associate editors to stay on, which has maintained quality and eased my worries.

We say good-bye to seven outgoing board members, Maurice Apprey, Linda Brakel, Henry Evans, Richard Gottlieb, Linda Mayes, Joy Osofsky, and Ellen Rees, and trust that they have gained as much as they have contributed. We will miss them and hope that they look forward to serving again in the future. Welcome to new board members J. Stuart Ablon, Richard Almond, Anne Erreich, Nathan Kravis, DonaldMoss, Shelley Orgel, and Steven Roose. They bring to JAPA a wealth of skills, knowledge, and broad experience, essential as we reach out to adjacent disciplines for the kind of dialogue that will enrich psychoanalysis as part of the vibrant intellectual climate of our times.

5

[[RunningHead]]

The Lawsuit and Democratic Procedures

Arnold Cooper

Arnold Cooper

285 Central Park West, Apt. 7S
New York, NY 10024-3006
Fax: 212-595-5225
E-mail: amcooper@bway.net

Arnold Cooper  

April 12, 2004. Recent articles by Robert Wallerstein and Richard Simons in JAPA (51/Supplement) and the publication in 1998 of Wallerstein's Lay Analysis: Life Inside the Controversy prompt me to add a note to the discussions of “the lawsuit” and the opening of training to nonphysicians by the institutes of the American. I was president of the American from 1980 to 1982, a time when the issue of nonmedical training regularly came before the Council, the Board, and the Executive Committee. The proposals that were put forward then were confined to accepting individuals for training in the institutes, without an accompanying resolution that after graduation they would be eligible for membership in the American on the same basis as physician graduates. It was my position that the training of nonphysicians as psychoanalysts was itself desirable (the entire history of psychoanalysis had demonstrated that medical training was not a prerequisite for excellence as a psychoanalytic clinician or theoretician), but if it was to occur at institutes of the American it had to be linked to a change in membership criteria, eliminating the requirement of a medical degree. This would require a bylaws change by a vote of the membership.

My urging that opening training must be linked to opening membership regularly produced demur within Council and Board and led to the seemingly endless appointment of study groups to weigh the problem. The necessary bylaws amendment to change our membership requirement was not put forward (probably out of concern that it would be defeated). Instead, the Gaskill proposal, which spoke only to the training issue and not to the membership issue, was passed. A few years later the gross injustice of graduating people who could not have the same access to membership as physicians became apparent, and we then of course amended our bylaws to accept nonmedical graduates.

I believe this important issue has been scanted in our history, and it has appeared as if some of us were opposing training for psychologists when in fact we were trying to protect the right of the members of a democratic organization to determine its identity. This issue of proper attention to our governance has recently reemerged.

Reflections on Clinical Theory

Insight and Interpretation: The Essential Tools of Psycho Analysis. By Roy Schafer New York: Other Press, 2003, 179 pp., $40.00.

Bad Feelings: Selected Psychoanalytic Essays. By Roy Schafer New York: Other Press, 2003, 180 pp., $40.00.

Sidney Phillips

Sidney Phillips

234 Church Street, Suite 300
New Haven, CT 06510-1802
Fax: 203-865-8311
E-mail: sidney.phillips@yale.edu

Sidney Phillips  

These extremely readable books by Roy Schafer address the major conceptual and technical problems encountered by adult psychoanalysts. They are excellent introductions to the field for the beginning analyst, while also providing seasoned analysts a concise and cogent review of the central themes of this prolific analytic writer's professional interests: ego psychology, unconscious fantasy, internalization, action language, narrative and story, and his recent explication of the neo-Kleinian perspective—all within a hermeneutic framework.

Insight and Interpretation is divided into three sections. The first, Insight and Its Vicissitudes, devotes its first three chapters to exploring the nature of insight. Here I found myself intrigued that Schafer, whose analytic roots extend deeply into the ego psychology tradition, immediately takes up Betty Joseph's landmark article, “On Understanding and Not Understanding” (1983), in which she explores the various meanings that analysands unconsciously attribute to insight. Schafer elucidates the psychoanalytic paradox that while analysands present for analytic treatment, whose hallmark is the development of understanding that will help relieve suffering, it soon emerges that they are ambivalent about just such a process. Some may for a long time feel too mistrustful and humiliated to embrace a dependency on the analyst that requires them to examine and speak intolerable impulses, desires, and perceived shortcomings. Insight itself can be a threat to some patients, and if this goes unaddressed, the treatment can founder.

271

Mariam Cohen  

Patterns is a difficult book to read. Marilyn Charles's vision is a grand and all-encompassing one, but—like impressionist art—is best viewed at a distance. Up close the picture lacks details and becomes fuzzy. Likewise, Charles presents an intriguing view of how the vicissitudes of early development—intensity of affect and affective attunement with caretakers particularly—inf luence later life and are played out in the analytic relationship. However, when one examines the details of her presentation, it comes across as lacking clarity and precision. Her writing is decidedly more poetic than scientific. Her style is allusive and evocative, perhaps as much an example as an explication of what she is trying to communicate. She is often repetitive and tends to wander, so that a reader is not sure how each chapter's topic differs from that of a preceding chapter. This style gives the book a disorganized appearance, suggesting that it may have benefited from a good editing.

Charles's argument is that we all experience early life in “patterns”: “The preverbal world is one of sensation, in which meanings accrue though the regularities and disregularities in our experience” (p. 1). In addition, even in later life traumatic memories are encoded primarily as these sensorimotor patterns, rather than verbally and symbolically. Affect is experienced primarily as regularity or disruption of soothing regularity in these patterns of sensation, and overwhelming affect becomes a traumatic disruption so that experiences associated with intense affect cannot be “known” in verbal, symbolic ways.

Charles relies heavily on Daniel Stern's conceptualization of development (1985)

Pleasures of Mind

Paul Schwaber

Paul Schwaber

258 Bradley Street
New Haven, CT 06511
Fax: 203-248-9856
E-mail: pschwaber@wesleyan.edu

Paul Schwaber   Professor of Letters, Wesleyan University; faculty, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis.

Often when I meet people socially for the first time, I'll be asked, “How did you come to have two professions?” Well—as this audience can well imagine—it's a long story. Usually I mumble something about narrative and fascination and try to shift the attention back to them. Today's occasion offers a chance to reflect about my two professions, literary studies and psychoanalysis, what drew me to them, and how they overlap and integrate. One emotional truth governs for me. They are sources of pleasure: more exactly, intellectual, moral, at times sober and dreadful, at others humorous, playful, anxious, or conflicted, often complex pleasures—in short, sublimatory pleasures. Both professions are alike in that. They allow me ranging joys of thought in the work I do, even when the work itself may involve distress.

For a long time, my path to them was more serendipitous than chosen. The youngest in a lively, affable, verbal family; an identified Jew emerging to consciousness during World War II, the discovery of the camps, and the struggle to protect the fledgling State of Israel, all the while safely ensconced in America, where Jews were a minority but hardly threatened; and an adolescent tacking through average-expectable adventures and turmoils at a huge public high school in Brooklyn after intense grade school years in a small Zionist yeshivah—I had a lot to fathom, a lot to be puzzled and curious about. When I was a child, I asked so many questions my father would call me Pinocchio. I was that nosy. And nosiness (since the ego is first of all a body ego) is a good word for it, the spark that energizes my fascination, my continuing pleasure, in figuring out what's going on and whether I have a sufficient take on it.

In college it took only brief exposure to Chaucer's Canterbury pilgrims, Wordsworth's poetry, and introductory physics to convince me

Talk given at the CORST Discussion Group, Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 22, 2004. An earlier version was presented to the Western New England Psychoanalytic Society, Novermber 1, 2003. Submitted for publication January 22, 2004.

In Creative Characters Elisabeth Young-Bruehl (1991) distinguished three foci of the ever growing body of literature devoted to the study of creativity: First, there was the “creative process”—the focus of inquiries into the cognitive components, mental states (spontaneity, independence from convention, and so on), and stages of production that result in a creative work. Next, there was creative ability—not only the raw talent, but the sublimatory and related capacities that support our universal ideal of the creative individual. (Forming a subcategory of this second focus were the environmental conditions—educational, cultural, social, and political—that favor or disfavor the creative impulse.) Finally, there was the “creative personality”—the target of investigations into conscious and unconscious motivation.

630

Silvia Bell   Training and Supervising Analyst and Associate Supervising Child Analyst, Baltimore-Washington Institute for Psychoanalysis.

The last fifteen years have seen major developments in psychoanalytic theory and practice. Not only is contemporary psychoanalysis “marked by a pluralism unknown in any prior era” (Gabbard and Westen 2003), but psychoanalysts from a classical, ego psychological perspective have undertaken a process of intense reexamination of traditional conceptualizations of female development, gender, homosexuality, culture, and technique.1 Given that Muriel Dimen's book was advertised as offering a psychoanalytic view of sexuality that is new and clinically meaningful, I approached her work with great interest.

This volume, No. 22 in the Relational Perspectives Book Series, is a compilation of nine papers, revised and annotated, that appeared originally between 1989 and 2001. As stated in a prologue, Dimen has assigned herself the ambitious task of breaking through orthodox ways of thinking about “gender and sexuality, about body, mind, and society, about the problem of dualism” (p. 2). She aims to take us beyond what she sees as the reductive/deterministic view of binary distinctions such as mind/body, male/female, psyche/society. Her fervor—and I label it such because there is a militancy to her determination in these pages— stems from her conviction that dualism masks a power hierarchy that imposes a value judgment: confronted with a binary, we are drawn to evaluate the two polarities. Hence, she embraces postmodernism, which “marks the voyage from dualism to multiplicity” as it “privileges difference, fragmentation, and indeterminacy” (p. 30), in opposition to modernism's rationalism and commitment to an absolute truth.

1See, for example, the table of contents of the two major publication representative of mainstream North American psychoanalysis in the last decade—the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association and Psychoanalytic Quarterly— to appreciate the extent and significance of this endeavor.651

Anita Katz   faculty, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, Metropolitan Institute for Training in Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy, and the object Relation Institute.

Wolfenstein and Leites noted more than fifty years ago that “the psychological study of cinematic art might be just as fruitful as Freud's applications of analytic thinking to the plays of Ibsen, Shakespeare and Sophocles” (cited in Gabbard 2001, p. 2). But a wholehearted endorsement of that view by the psychoanalytic community has been a long time coming. Wolfenstein's study of Kubrick's A Clockwork Orange (1976) is a gem, and there are other such gems in our literature going back many years. Psychoanalytic Review has published articles on movies since the sixties. But uncertainty about how to use film material, and possibly Freud's skepticism about the medium, left the movies largely outside of psychoanalytic purview, a vastly neglected resource. Happily, this is increasingly less true.

The work of Glen Gabbard (2001; Gabbard and Gabbard 1999), including seven methodologies for the study of film (2001, pp. 5-12), has no doubt contributed to the legitimization of psychoanalytic film study. The International Journal of Psychoanalysis now has a section devoted to movies, and both JAPA and Psychoanalytic Psychology have recently published movie-inspired papers. The growing number of film-related projects, and the number of analysts participating in them, further attest film's new status in our field.

This is as it should be. Gabbard (2001) calls films “a storehouse for the psychological images of our time” (p. 3). He believes, and I agree, that film, like the theater in ancient Greece, is the art form that can be shared and enjoyed by all the citizens of its time—men and women, old and young, rich and poor (Knox 1994).

673

Lacan

Phenomenology and Lacan on Schizophrenia, After the Decade of the Brain. By Alphonse De Waelhens and Wilfried Ver Eecke. Leuven: Leuven University Press, 2001, 337 pp., $62.50 paperback.

Stanley Possick

Stanley Possick

102 Marvel Road
New Haven, CT 06515
E-mail: stanley.possick@yale.edu

Stanley Possick   Faculty, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis; Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Yale University School of Medicine.

Phenomenology and Lacan on Schizophrenia, after the Decade of the Brain is an ambitious, complex, scholarly, and well-written book. It consists of two distinct but interrelated parts. The latter two-thirds of the text is a republication of Wilfried Ver Eecke's 1978 English translation of Alphonse De Waelhens's Schizophrenia, published originally in French as La Psychose (1972). De Waelhens studies psychotic behavior from phenomenological and philosophical perspectives, informed by the psychoanalytic work of Freud and Lacan. He expresses the hope that such interdisciplinary work will “clarify the potentiality for madness lying at the very heart of the structures that are constitutive of the human condition” (p. 12). His work interpreting Lacan's conceptualization of schizophrenia has long been considered a seminal contribution (see Lemaire 1977). Since the original publication of De Waelhens's book, the discovery of additional seminars by Lacan and historical research on Schreber's life have strengthened De Waelhens's theory of schizophrenia. These factors, coupled with Ver Eecke's belief that De Waelhens's and Lacan's understanding of the psychic structure of the schizophrenic has significant clinical utility, justify the republication of Schizophrenia.

The first part of the book is a lengthy monograph, written recently by Ver Eecke and dedicated to the memory of his teacher De Waelhens. It prepares the reader for De Waelhens's text, and stands on its own as a work of serious scholarship. Ver Eecke is addressing an English-speaking audience, which consists both of non-Lacanian psychoanalysts, like me, who have little or no knowledge of Lacan's or De Waelhens's work, and Lacanian psychoanalysts, who have an interest in work with psychotic patients. My review will focus on Ver Eecke's contribution.

991

Delia Battin   Training and Supervising Analyst, Child and Adult Programs, New York Freudian Society and Institute.

Psychoanalysis, in its attempts to probe the human mind in all its genetic and current dimensions and complexities, its oedipal conflicts, its preoedipal identity crises, is faced with tangled riddles as seemingly unravelable as the Sphynxian one that confronted Oedipus. How does a neonate emerge from a preobject world into what Mahler called the psychological birth of the human infant? That topic alone has been the subject of books, debates, and controversies, but if it can be agreed on that after the twin tasks of attachment and individuation have been engaged by the human child in the first eighteen months of life, and somewhat resolved in the next eighteen, another challenge faces the infant, which is the subject matter of this remarkable book. A human being does not merely have to attach to his or her parents and then develop a sense of individuation from them. A developing psyche does not merely have to engage the paradox of independent selfhood emerging from a learned trust in utter dependency on initial nurturing objects. No, there is more.

1010

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ethics and Religion

The Ethic of Honesty: The Fundamental Rule of Psychoanalysis. By M. Guy Thompson. Amsterdam: Rodopi Press, 2004, xxii + 169 pp., $36.00.

Stanley Leavy

Stanley Leavy

200 Leeder Hill Drive
Hamden, CT 06517
E-mail: stanleavy@aol.com

Stanley Leavy   Training and Supervising Analyst (retired), Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry Emeritus, Yale University School of Medicine.

The premise of this book is a sharp distinction drawn between free association and the fundamental rule, by which the analysand is charged to speak whatever comes to mind. Ordinarily the two ideas are spoken of as if synonymous, despite their logical asymmetry. Free association is a technical procedure, objectively definable; the basic rule is an injunction, in M. Guy Thompson's view an ethical injunction, openly or tacitly accepted as such by the analysand. As such, the rule “say whatever comes to mind” accentuates the word “whatever,” implying that any choice of expression will be confronted by inner limitations opposing honest expression. It is the emphasized word that sets the philosophical tone for the book: Thompson, an experienced analyst now affiliated with the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California, presents a view of psychoanalysis as a quest for inner freedom through what might be called training in honesty.

The overriding character of Thompson's thought is phenomenological; it is rooted in that school of philosophy, in which the lived experience is paramount. Although the philosophical elaborations of phenomenology are far from simple, its application to psychoanalysis is readily comprehensible. If we can define biology as the investigation of organisms, phenomenology is the investigation of appearances, that is, of things as they appear. What appear in a psychoanalytic session are the thoughts of the analysand—“whatever comes to mind”— mediated through words, but also confined by intentions of which the analysand may not be aware. This, together with the corresponding attention of the analyst, is the analytic situation as experienced—a sufficient description only if we add to it the emotional reality of both participants.

Thompson wrestles throughout the book with questions about the use the analyst makes of what the patient says, questions that reflect the analyst's personal emotional responses and attitudes, as well as attitudes derived from training. Traditionally, although not always consistently, the analyst is enjoined to maintain a neutral attitude toward all the analysand's expressions—but what does this neutrality amount to?

[[RunningHead]]

Abstracts of the 2005 Poster Session of the American Psychoanalytic Association Winter Meeting

Therapist-Patient Interaction Rating Scale (TPIRS): Instrument Development and Preliminary Reliability

Jorge Cassab , Frank Yeomans and John Clarkin

John Clarkin

Murillo 34-1
Col. Mixcoac
Mexico City, MEXICO 03700
E-mail: doccassab@mac.com

John Clarkin  

The Therapist-Patient Interaction Rating Scale instrument (TPIRS) is derived from Kernberg's Transference Focused Psychotherapy (TFP) for treating Borderline Personality Organizations (BPO) (Kernberg 1984). With the transference interpretation as the key intervention, the main objective of treatment is to integrate and consolidate the personality structure of the subject (Kernberg 1984). Emphasis is placed on ego functioning, reality testing, sense of identity, and defensive mechanisms, all which can be evaluated via the subject's verbal and nonverbal language during the therapy sessions. Because these elements are worked with and constantly evaluated in each session throughout the different stages of treatment, the clinician's experience is important when learning and utilizing TFP.

The TPIRS is a dual-scale instrument that dynamically (minute by minute) rates therapist interventions and patient responses on a 0-5 scale (Cassab 2004; Clarkin, Yeomans, and Kernberg 1999). As can be seen in Table 1, listing therapist interventions, the instrument includes detailed working definitions of these interventions.

This allows the rater to look for specific interventions as they happen. The scale moves from silence to more structured interventions and on to more abstract and elaborate interventions.

The second perspective in this instrument, patient responses, presents a series of observable dynamics noticeable in the patient according to the realm of response. Behaviors as such are assumed to reflect underlying intrapsychic defense mechanisms, which may be inferred (see Table 2)

The objectives of our instrument development and preliminary reliability study were (1) to create an instrument simultaneously rating therapist interventions and patient responses as they occur in real time throughout a TFP treatment; and (2) to test the reliability of the instrument by making it accessible to other researchers for them to apply with their own subjects and with therapeutic modalities other than TFP.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Abstracts of the 2005 Poster Session of the American Psychoanalytic Association Winter Meeting

Changes in Personality During the First Six Months of Analysis Using a Clinician Q-Sort Measure: A Preliminary Report

Rosemary Cogan and John Porcerelli

John Porcerelli

Department of Psychology Texas Tech University
Lubbock, TX 79409-2051
E-mail: r.cogan@ttu.edu

John Porcerelli  

One of the challenges of assessing psychoanalytic outcome is the problem of applying measures that are well established, meet conventional standards of reliability and validity, assess features that are truly meaningful, and meet pragmatic requirements. Shedler and Westen (1998) have developed the Shedler-Westen Assessment Procedure-200 (SWAP200), a dynamically informed Q-sort measure to which the clinician responds by rank ordering 200 statements, from those “very descriptive” of an individual patient to those “inapplicable or not descriptive.” Westen and Shedler have argued eloquently that clinicians are sophisticated observers of personality and have discussed the importance of longitudinal studies of the effectiveness of treatments as actually practiced in the community (Westen, Novotny, and Thompson-Brenner 2004).

In a preliminary cross-sectional study we compared psychoanalysts’ SWAP-200 descriptions of patients beginning and patients ending psychoanalysis (Cogan and Porcerelli 2005). We concluded that the SWAP-200 is a useful and practical way of studying longitudinal changes in people during psychoanalysis. We are now beginning a longitudinal study of psychoanalysis with the SWAP-200. This is a report of our early findings.

Method

Twenty-two psychoanalyst volunteers have each provided a SWAP-200 description of one patient in psychoanalysis, and have each agreed to describe their patient, using the same method, every six months until the analysis reaches a natural conclusion. Here we report the changes in 14 of these patients during the first six months of their analysis. The SWAP-200 Q-sorts were scored for (1) personality disorders, (2) personality strengths, and (3) the most characteristic SWAP-200 items.

Findings

Six months into psychoanalysis, there were very modest changes in personality characteristics. Scores on a scale of narcissistic traits were lower (p =.06). Scores on two measures of personality strengths were unchanged.

[[RunningHead]]

Abstracts of the 2005 Poster Session of the American Psychoanalytic Association Winter Meeting

Lisa and David: Two Patients’ Differing Retrospective Views of Psychotherapy Effectiveness

Morris Roy and Elena di Modrone

Elena di Modrone

2220 Ocean Avenue
Brooklyn, NY 11229
E-mail: MMorrisroy@aol.com

IPTAR

Elena di Modrone  

This study looks intensively at two patients who differ markedly in their self-reported post-treatment view of their therapy. David reports 1325 “relatively high satisfaction,” while Lisa reports being “largely dissatisf ied” with therapy. Three dimensions are looked at in relation to satisfaction: (1) attachment style as measured by the Reciprocal Attachment Questionnaire, or RAQ (West and Sheldon-Keller 1994), adapted for use with the therapist functioning as an attachment figure); (2) capacity for self-reflection as measured by the Reflective Functioning Scale, or RF (Fonagy et al. 1998); and (3) ways in which representations of the therapeutic dialogue are internalized, measured by the Representation of the Therapeutic Dialogue, or RTD (Geller et al. 2005).

We hypothesize that attachment, reflective functioning, and ways in which the object relationship with the therapist is internalized are all related to the individual's experience of treatment as largely “effective” or “ineffective.”

Method

In 1996, ninety-nine clinic patients from the Institute of Psychoanalytic Training and Research (IPTAR) Clinical Center completed Seligman's Effectiveness Questionnaire, or EQ (Seligman 1995), assessing self-reported satisfaction.

[[RunningHead]]

Abstracts of the 2005 Poster Session of the American Psychoanalytic Association Winter Meeting

Using Art to Teach About the Holocaust

Mia Biran and Edna Southard

Edna Southard

122 Benton Hall
Miami University
Oxford, OH 45056
E-mail: biranmw@muohio.edu

Edna Southard  

In discussing art as a pedagogical tool, Biran and Hill (2004) concluded that engagement of the arts in the college curriculum would provide a creative involvement of students in the learning process and would lead to a higher degree of understanding of meanings (Moyers 1999). This method of teaching may be of special importance in teaching courses on psychopathology, where the subject matter is often complex and abstract.

This paper focuses on our teaching a course called “Generations of the Holocaust” as an aspect of the more general topic of trauma. Typically, the class consists of forty students, three-quarters of them female. They do not differ in any aspect from the rest of the Psychology Department's junior and senior undergraduates. Most students in this course had very little knowledge about the events of the Holocaust. They had difficulty grasping the horrors of the Holocaust, its psychological impact on the survivors, and the subtle ways in which the associated trauma was transmitted to succeeding generations. The first author strug gled with the difficulty of explaining such abstract concepts as repression, dissociation, and splitting—central defense mechanisms observed to be used by severely traumatized patients (Herman 1992). This teaching challenge led to the idea of bringing these concepts closer to students’ intellectual and emotional comprehension by involving them in actively viewing artistic expressions of trauma.

Method

In spring 2004, the Art Museum of Miami University presented an exhibition of recent paintings by Vera Klement, a prominent artist and childhood survivor of the Holocaust. Her paintings speak loudly about pain and loss. The forty students enrolled in “Generations of the Holocaust” were asked to walk through the gallery and ref lect on their feelings, associations, and impressions. They then each voiced these reactions and pointed to the painting that had made the greatest impact on them. The second author, herself a childhood survivor of the Holocaust, as well as curator of the museum, engaged the students in an open and extensive discussion about their reactions. The three paintings chosen most often by the students were (1) Fallen Chimney (2000)

Psychoanalysis and (Auto)Biography

Gustav Mahler: A Life in Crisis. By Stuart Feder. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2004, 368 pp., $39.95.

Gregory Graham

Gregory Graham

4550 Post Oak Place, Suite 300
Houston, TX 77027
Fax: 713-622-3888
E-mail: gregorydgraham@hotmail.com

Gregory Graham   Training and Supervising Analyst, Houston-Galveston Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Baylor College of Medicine.

One does not need to be a Mahler devotee or a psychoanalyst to appreciate Stuart Feder's Gustav Mahler: A Life in Crisis, which gives us fresh reason to consider Mahler in the light of his music, his times, and his psychology. Feder, who is an analyst and a musician, uses his expertise in both fields to weave a fascinating study of the interplay between Mahler's personal life and the music he composed. The story he tells is complicated and engrossing, made more compelling by Feder's painstaking research of the historical and musical material and by his clear, modest, and engaging style. He does not go to speculative excess when interpreting musical passages or personal events, even when the material might tempt a less conscientious scholar to rhapsodize with abandon or presume “certain” knowledge of the “true” depths of the personalities of Mahler and his contemporaries. These are no mean achievements for a biographer; Mahler was an astonishing and prolifically creative individual.

Clinical Theory and Practice

Le Divan Bien Tempéré (The Well-Tempered Couch). By Jean-Luc Donnet. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France, 1995, 320 pp., €28,50.

La Fin Du Divan? (End of the Couch?). By Raymond Cahn. Paris: Odile Jacob, 2002, 259 pp., €24,50.

Emmett Wilson

Emmett Wilson

943 Canal Road
Princeton NJ 08540
Fax: 908-904-1558
E-mail: ewilsonj@ix.netcom.com

Emmett Wilson   Editorial Board, Psychoanalytic Quarterly.

These two volumes focus on the relation between the psychoanalytic process and the psychoanalytic setting. Both authors, Jean-Luc Donnet and Raymond Cahn, try to define the frame within which psychoanalysis takes place. They examine its psychological function, its constitution, and the degree of permissible flexibility in the setting that is consistent with or necessary for the unfolding of an analytic process.

Both authors must begin with Lacan, if only to repudiate him. They are in agreement that the fervent questioning of theory and practice that began with Lacan has over the past several decades made French psychoanalysis a vibrant force and a “dominant pole” in the world psychoanalytic movement. Cahn, however, cites a pointed comment by Pontalis: “Each one of us has been at one time close to Lacan, each one has disengaged himself from him, and all of us say: at such and such a date, one could not but be Lacanian, and after such and such a date, one could be Lacanian no longer. But the dates each of us would give are not at all the same” (p. 249; translation mine, here and throughout). Cahn himself sums up Lacan's influence: “The schism provoked by [Lacan's] practice of treatment—unacceptable for all other [non-Lacanian] analysts—had, however, two other particular consequences: the one, happy, of stimulating, to the extreme, research and reflection on practice and theory, among his partisans as well as among his adversaries.

1005

Reply to Madelon Sprengnether

Roy Schafer

Roy Schafer

390 West End Avenue #6F
New York, NY 10024
Fax: 212-877-4099
E-mail: royschafer@mindspring.com

Roy Schafer  

I regret that I did not clearly distinguish forgiveness and waiving of forgiveness, and so led Madelon Sprengnether to be uncertain of my meaning. I am grateful to her thoughtful reading of my paper, and to the editor, for affording me this further opportunity to clarify a distinction between forgiveness and unforgiveness that I was attempting to make.

By “waiving of forgiveness” I intended to convey the rational action one would take upon realizing that he or she had been active in provoking the painful situation in which another person seemed to be the sole perpetrator. Lear might seem to be the sole perpetrator were one to interpret Cordelia's “Nothing, my lord” in any way other than her expressing anger and a desire to be hurtful at the time of the love contest, and her doing so ambivalently, that is, despite her loving, dutiful, and protective feelings for Lear, feelings all the more intense in view of his mental and emotional decline. I tried to point to a number of lines throughout the play's first scene that, taken together, seem to show that Shakespeare was preparing the ground for Cordelia's ultimate “No cause, no cause.” A complex, multiple view of responsibility for pain and disaster often, as here, precludes any one participant's feeling it appropriate to assume a forgiving attitude toward the other(s). In this case, forgiveness is no longer at issue, though, unconsciously, unforgiving feelings may persist. I have tried to show that Shakespeare carefully constructed act I, scene i, to prepare his audience to realize that both Lear and Cordelia, loving as they were but each being in a complex developmental and emotional situation, jointly brought about a tragic course of events that culminated in their both perishing.

Then, in my final discussion of forgiveness, I reiterated my belief in one's being capable of experiencing and extending forgiveness on higher levels of function, while reasserting my belief that in response to significant abuse one is bound to remain unconsciously irreconcilable.

David Joseph

David Joseph   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, George Washington University; Training and Supervising Analyst, Washington Psychoanalytic Institute.

Given the emphasis on conf identiality in the recent psychoanalytic literature, it may come as a real surprise to learn that the first legal proceeding involving a psychotherapeutic breach of conf identiality did not occur until 1952, and that the word confidentiality did not appear in any psychoanalytic journal written in English until 1961. Although confidentiality was always implicitly essential to the conduct of psychoanalysis, a review of the bibliography of this volume indicates that it did not become the focus of significant attention until the writings of Martin Stein and Robert Stoller in the late 1980s. Even now, we are more likely to attend to the causes and consequences of violations of conf identiality than to the conditions necessary to its establishment and maintenance.

This volume grew out of a conference held in Montreal in October 2000, and in their introduction the editors note the importance of two events in their decision to organize this meeting: (1) the publication of The New Informants by Bollas and Sundelson in 1995 and (2) the ruling of the U.S. Supreme Court in Jaffee v Redmond in 1996. In their book, Bollas and Sundelson, both of whom have contributed chapters to the present volume, detail the erosion of psychoanalytic confidentiality, argue for the necessity of absolute confidentiality for psychoanalysis, and propose the establishment of a “new profession,” the social therapist. Social therapists would enter an advocacy relationship with their clients and, in addition to exploring their clients' problems and feelings, would be required to report criminal activities. Psychoanalysts, by contrast, would be forbidden from breaking confidentiality. In the case of Jaffee v Redmond, Karen Beyer, a psychotherapist, treated a policewoman, Mary Lu Redmond, who had shot and killed a man while on duty. Both Beyer and Redmond were employed by the same village.

1023

Musing on Forgiveness: A Response to Roy Schafer

Madelon Sprengnether

Madelon Sprengnether

207 Church Street
University of Minnesota

Madelon Sprengnether   Professor of English, University of Minnesota.

King Lear, a play of exceptional harshness and brutality, in which even the gods seem to enjoy the spectacle of human suffering— “As flies to wanton boys are we to th’ gods,” says Edgar; “They kill us for their sport” (IV.i.36-37)—is also a play without mothers.

Queen Lear has been so long absent that she is never alluded to— almost as if Lear's daughters, like Athena, had sprung full-grown from their father's brain. Edmund's mother, the subject of the conversation between Kent and Gloucester that opens the play, is mentioned blushingly by Gloucester, who had “good sport” (I.i.22) with her in the conceiving of his bastard son Edmund, but she herself does not appear. The legitimate Edgar, who refers to Edmund's mother only by reference to her sexuality—“The dark and vicious place where thee he got” (V.iii.173)—says nothing about his own maternal heritage. In a world of men and tough-minded daughters, who enact the worst stereotypes of masculine self-interest and aggression, Cordelia stands out—both in her refusal to lie for personal gain and in her capacity for loving forgiveness. Her “No cause, no cause” (IV.vii.75) in response to Lear's acknowledgment that she has reason to wish him harm, appears to wipe clean the slate of Lear's wrongdoing. How does she arrive at such a transcendent capacity for forgiveness—or does she? This is the question Roy Schafer poses in his subtle and thought-provoking essay.

I raise the question of mothers because Schafer invokes them in his reference to Melanie Klein (1946) and the importance of the depressive position (where hatred and aggression can be held as internal objects, instead of being split off and projected onto a persecuting other) for the achievement of the ability to forgive, as well as the desire to make reparation for one's own destructive wishes and impulses. Implicitly, in Klein's paradigm (as more explicitly in object relations theory)

Commentary on Ablon and Jones

Sidney Blatt

Sidney Blatt

25 Park Street
New Haven, CT 06519
Fax: 203-785-7357
E-mail: sidney.blatt@yale.edu

Sidney Blatt   Professor of Psychiatry and Psychology and Chief of the Psychology Section in the Department of Psychiatry, Yale University; faculty, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis.

Stuart Ablon and Enrico Jones, in this target paper, address an issue central to contemporary psychoanalytic theory and practice—the def inition of psychoanalytic process. Some investigators (e.g., Vaughan et al. 1997) report difficulty arriving at a consensus def inition of what practicing psychoanalysts mean by “psychoanalytic process.” Difficulty def ining something so basic and fundamental may reflect the remarkable diversity of contemporary psychoanalytic thought and practice. But this lack of agreement about such a central construct creates serious problems for contemporary clinical practice, training, and research. Thus, one has to admire the ef forts of the late Enrico Jones, over many years, and of his student and protégé, Stuart Ablon, to address this vitally important question, and to do so systematically and rigorously—to try to define the psychoanalytic process empirically and to test the implications of this definition by comparing different forms of psychotherapeutic intervention to this definition. Ablon and Jones have also developed a method, based on Q-sort methodology, for identifying patterns of therapeutic interaction in specific patient-therapist dyads. This enables them to investigate different patterns of interaction in the therapeutic process and their role in therapeutic outcome.

Ablon and Jones use a well-established conceptual and statistical methodology, one particularly suited for identifying variations among individuals or among processes rather than identifying differences among variables—the Q-methodology originally conceptualized and developed by William Stephenson in the 1950s based on his experiences with the British statistician Sir Cyril Burt.

569

What are these stories I described as central that have raised Joseph Schachter's ire? They are “the stories remembered but never integrated; the stories experienced but never formulated; the stories experienced and remembered only in the language of action; the stories of uncon258scious fantasy and defense; and the importance of all these in every other story” (p. 25).

In short, these stories are the representations of the dynamic unconscious, the core of psychoanalysis since its inception, and the one component of psychic structure considered central in symptom formation by all schools of psychoanalysis.1 It hardly seems to me to be a “personal” or “unqualified” conclusion. Psychoanalysts don’t differ in their belief in the patient's need to tell and own stories but in (1) what stories we sometimes consider central (e.g., Kleinian or self psychological) and (2) what methods we emphasize in describing how we help patients own their stories (e.g., interpreting countertransference, empathic failures, drive derivatives, etc.).

For over a hundred years the vast majority of analysts of different stripes have listened and tried to understand their patients’ stories. While Schachter disputes this telling and owning of stories as central, he wants his “knowledge” of uncertainty to be central. Here, as so often seems the case, we have the hidden authoritarian claim behind the plea for diversity.

Chilling is the way Schachter casually distorts the clinical material.

1Even relational analysts, who often give the impression of rejecting thisperspective, have vigorously disputed this, as in the recent panel “Relational Perspectives in Psychoanalyis” (JAPA51/1).258

History and Psychoanalysis

Solitary Sex: A Cultural History of Masturbation. By Thomas Laqueur New York: Zone Books, 2003, 501 pp., $34.00.

Aaron Esman

Aaron Esman

115 East 86th Street
New York, NY 10028
Fax: 212-722-1970
E-mail: ahesman@earthlink.net

Aaron Esman  

Psychoanalysis has been concerned with the question of masturbation since the earliest days of Freud's explorations into the mysteries of human sexuality and its relations with the neurotic disorders he was observing in his consulting room. His views, as well as those of his students and followers, changed and evolved over the decades, but the subject has never faded from psychoanalytic interest. It has, to one degree or another, been conceived as a central issue in both normal psychic development and pathogenesis.

It is Laqueur's thesis that this view of the importance of masturbation is of relatively recent origin, and he has produced a remarkably readable, witty, and scholarly study of its place in history from the classical era to the present in support of this position. In essence, he proposes that what he calls “modern masturbation”—the notion that “solitary sex” is both physically and morally corrupting and potentially lethal to both sexes—dates only from the early eighteenth century, “around 1712.”

In or near that fateful year there appeared on the burgeoning London literary market an anonymous screed bearing the title “Onania, or, the Heinous Sin of Self Pollution, and all its Frightful Consequences, in both SEXES Considered, with Spiritual and Physical Advice to those who have already injured themselves by this abominable practice. And seasonable Admonition to the Youth of Both Sexes.” The author, whom Laqueur has identified as a notorious quack and pornographer named James Marten, graciously offered for sale a number of medicaments that, he assured his readers, would surely cure them of this dreadful vice.

325

Golan Shahar  

September 15, 2004. We appreciate Jonathan Rosenfeld's careful reading of our article and his pointing out the importance of examining pretreatment differences between psychoanalysis (PSA) and support-ive-expressive psychotherapy (SEP) in our further analyses of data from the Menninger Psychotherapy Research Project. It should be noted, however, that this had been done in an earlier analysis of this data set (Blatt 1992) and no signif icant pretreatment differences were found, either between the two treatment groups (PSA and SEP) or between the two groups of patients (anaclitic and introjective) on the Health-Sickness Rating Scale (Luborsky 1962). Nor was there a significant difference in the length of treatment, though by design patients in PSA were seen significantly more frequently each week (M = 4.67) than patients in SEP (M = 2.72). Additionally, no significant differences were found between the two treatment groups (PSA and SEP) on any of the outcome measures used in our analyses of these data, though indications suggest that introjective patients at pretreatment seemed more disturbed than anaclitic patients (they had significantly more thought disorder and greater investment in unrealistic [inaccurately perceived] object representations).

While the findings in this earlier report appear to address Rosenfeld's concern about the possibility of confounds in this non-randomized study, we believe that his observation has a more general implication for the often poorly understood nature of psychotherapy trials. Specifically, he observes that “in a study that is not randomized, it is possible that multiple factors may be distributed unequally among the groups being compared.” What many research consumers tend to overlook is the fact that even in a randomized trial, multiple factors might differentiate the groups. In other words, the success of randomization is probabilistic in nature, and should never be taken for granted. Further, even if randomization is successful, it does not eliminate the possibility of a certain factor interacting with the treatment conditions in predicting outcomes. It is this very possibility that served as our incentive to examine patient-by-treatment interactions, an examination that, we believe, yielded fascinating results about the differential effects of psychoanalysis and supportive-expressive psychotherapy with anaclitic and introjective patients.

Ethics and Religion

W. Meissner

W. Meissner

St. Mary's Hall, Boston College
Chestnut Hill, MA 02467

W. Meissner   University Professor of Psychoanalysis, Boston College; faculty emeritus, Psychoanalytic Institute of New England, East.

Randall Lehmann Sorenson is professor of psychology at the Rosemead School of Psychology in La Mirada, California, and a training and supervising analyst at the Institute of Contemporary Psychoanalysis in Los Angeles. Upon reading the dust jacket of Minding Spirituality,

1032

I optimistically looked forward to a useful contribution, from an experienced analytic clinician and teacher, to the important matter of how analysts can work effectively and in authentic analytic ways with religious patients. My expectations were disappointed, however, since there is little in the book that addresses the complexity of this delicate and important matter in present-day psychoanalysis. Discussions of the pros and cons surrounding this debate are sparse, or at least sparser than I would have hoped. This may speak more to my own desires than to any deficiency in the book. Professor Sorenson did not have to write a book to suit my wishes.

Even so, I must say up front that to his credit he offers a message that I fully endorse and celebrate, namely, that the religious beliefs and convictions of our patients are not only fair game for analytic exploration and understanding, but that they are important aspects of the patient's psychic life that are eminently deserving of such exploration and understanding. Sorenson makes the case for openness and acceptance in the analyst toward the patient's belief system, but at the same time eschews blind endorsement as much as ready dismissal. What comes through in the clinical material he presents is the image of a sensitive, empathic, and thoughtful analyst, one conscious of and responsive to the religious aspects of the patient's anxieties and conflicts. As it turns out, the religious beliefs, attitudes, and practices of our patients can serve as vehicles for conflictual and maladaptive behavior just as well as any other human involvement. Religion has no prior claim to exemption or transcendent exclusion from analytic scrutiny. Thus far Sorenson and I are on the same side.

William Riess   Member analyst, C. G. Jung Institute of San Francisco; faculty, The Psychotherapy Institute, Berkeley

The vicissitudes of the relationship between Sabina Spielrein, C. G. Jung, and Sigmund Freud have generated significant interest ever since Aldo Carotenuto's publication in 1980 of Diario di un Segreta Simmetria: Sabina Spielrein tra Jung e Freud and its translation in 1982 as A Secret Symmetry: Sabina Spielrein between Jung and Freud. John Kerr's widely read A Most Dangerous Method. The Story of Jung, Freud and Sabina Spielrein (1993) heightened interest in this aspect of the history of psychoanalysis. Films and plays1 have capitalized on the universal themes linking that complicated threesome, and a number of psychoanalytic training centers have focused in their programs on the evident ethical and boundary issues between Jung as doctor and Spielrein as his patient.

Less evident and valued have been Spielrein's contributions to the development of the emerging field of psychoanalysis, some thirty-seven papers and notes, many not available in English. Spielrein was given credit by Freud in a footnote to Beyond the Pleasure Principle (1920, p. 1) for anticipating, in her “Destruction as a Cause of Coming into Being” (1912), his conceptualization of the death instinct. She had presented the paper to the Vienna Psychoanalytic Society in 1911, a month after becoming its second woman member.

1My Name Was Sabina Spielrein, Elizabeth Marton, director; Prendimi l'anima (Take My Soul), Roberto Faenza, director; Sabina by Willie Holtzman (1996); The Talking Cure by Christopher Hampton (2003).1349

[[RunningHead]]

The Lure of Hypocrisy

Anton Kris

Anton Kris

16 Channing Place
Cambridge, MA 02138-3307
E-mail: aok@kris.org

Anton Kris   Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute;Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School.

Why are we susceptible to hypocrisy? Four preconditions for falling prey to hypocrisy are delineated. The reasons for our complex attitude to hypocrisy, both condoning and condemning it, are also explored. Hypocrisy is the false claim to virtue. It always refers to consciously intended deception by a person in a position of trust. Making use of literary examples, the investigation starts with the delineation of three readily apparent preconditions for falling prey to hypocrisy. Idealization of the hypocrite is seen as a defense against a dread of uncertainty on the part of the person who succumbs to hypocrisy. The addition of a third precondition, the force of powerful desire, completes the introduction. A selective review of historical and philosophical studies of hypocrisy over the past twenty-five hundred years situates the problem of the susceptibility to hypocrisy. Application of psychoanalytic formulations and clinical data, principally from the study of patients whose psychoanalysts committed ethical violations, produces a fourth precondition for succumbing to hypocrisy: transference, with a regression from mature trust to a condition of unmodified basic trust.
