Samuel Herschkowitz  

A Spirit of Inquiry, by Joseph Lichtenberg, Frank Lachmann, and James Fosshage, reflects a complexity of thought belied by the book's modest size. A first reading of this monograph will be insufficient for the reader to appreciate fully the many intricate technical and theoretical issues dealt with here. This is the third volume collaborated on by these authors. While the other works approach different aspects of relational psychoanalysis, a reading of all three will help to elucidate the authors’ clinical and theoretical framework. In A Spirit of Inquiry, Lichtenberg, Lachmann, and Fosshage create a developmental schema to bolster their understanding of why a relational approach is central to the therapeutic action of psychoanalysis. The relational model they generate is unique and offers a complex understanding of how therapeutic action occurs. They integrate systems theory and a developmental model incorporating data from attachment research, neuroscience, self psychology, and child observation. The result is an intersubjective model whose richness results from its fine interconnections among various neighboring theories (e.g., self psychology), as well as from its own innovative aspects. A decade of collaboration among these three clinicians has produced a sophisticated and useful viewpoint within psychoanalytic theory.

306

Reply to Blatt and Fonagy

Stuart Ablon

Stuart Ablon

313 Washington Street, Suite 402
Newton, MA 02458

Stuart Ablon  

I would like to thank JAPA for inviting Sidney Blatt and Peter Fonagy to comment on our paper and for publishing the ensuing dialogue. It is indeed a special opportunity to have such luminaries in the field reflect openly on our work. We had an ambitious agenda for our target paper and recognize that we may not have been able to accomplish all of our missions simultaneously. Like Sidney Blatt, we hope that many readers may wish to learn more about the methods we describe in our two studies. For more information about the 100 items that constitute the Psychotherapy Process Q-set (PQS), I suggest reading Enrico Jones's exceptional book, Therapeutic Action (2000), which provides an excellent conceptual overview of the measure and contains its complete coding manual and 100 items. Blatt was also curious about the similarities and differences between our prototypes of analytic process and cognitive-behavioral process. For a thorough comparison of the prototypes, please see Ablon and Jones (1998), as that was indeed beyond the scope of this paper.

As Peter Fonagy points out, psychotherapy researchers face a daunting task trying to capture the tremendous depth of clinical encounters in a manner that can be subjected to quantitative analysis. For this reason, any empirical research into the process of psychoanalysis is an easy target for criticisms of superficiality and lack of depth. Psychoanalysis will suffer, though, if such criticisms deter researchers from at least attempting to capture complex phenomena in reliable empirical ways. The lack of an empirical (here I mean quantitative) foundation in psychoanalysis has left the field open to attack in an age in which clinicians, patients, and third-party payers all have heard about “empirically supported” treatments. Perhaps the most worrisome aspect of the empirically supported treatment movement is that many training programs in the United States today teach only treatment processes that have received empirical support in controlled clinical trials.

Analyzing Freud Letters of H.D., Bryher, and Their Circle. Edited by Susan Stanford Friedman. New York:, New Directions, 2002. 615. $39.95.

Sybil Houlding

Sybil Houlding

205 Church Street, Suite 405
New Haven, CT 06510

Sybil Houlding   Faculty, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis; Assistant Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Yale University School of Medicine.

This is a big book; in fact it is several books. It is at once the lively and revealing account, through letters, of the analysis that the poet Hilda Doolittle (H.D.) underwent with Freud for three months in 1933 and for five weeks in 1934—the “back story,” so to speak, to Tribute to Freud, H.D.'s well-known (if little read) memoir of her analysis. It is as well a vivid picture of the inner life and daily concerns of H.D. and her companion Bryher (the pen name of Annie Winifred Ellerman), and their circle of friends, many of whom became significant modernist writers. Friedman, an H.D. scholar whose work on the book was in part supported by a grant from the American Psychoanalytic Association, is well aware of the wide net her book casts, and its importance to both the psychoanalytic community and the academic community. She has successfully provided guidance to both groups.

Friedman has done a masterful job of bringing organization to what could otherwise feel like an overwhelming mass of historical material. She organizes the letters around the two periods of H.D.'s analysis, with a Prologue for the period leading up to the analysis, a section titled “Between the Acts” for the period between the two analyses, and an Epilogue. The letters between H.D. and Bryher during the first analysis she labels Act I. These are broken into weekly clusters. The letters in “Between the Acts” are divided into annual clusters. The letters in Act II—from the second analysis—are divided into two groups. Each week of letters, and each larger section, is preceded by Friedman's commentary—essays of wit and concision—that provide biographical, historical, aesthetic, and theoretical context for the letters that follow. These commentaries could well be read on their own.

1343

Early Developmental Issues Reconsidered

Commentary on Pine's Ideas on Symbiosis

Doris Silverman

Doris Silverman

315 Central Park West
New York, NY 10025
E-mail: dksilverman@aol.com

Doris Silverman   Training, Teaching, and Supervising Fellow, Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research (IPTAR); Adjunct Professor, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

The concept of symbiosis is a powerful one expressing a significant emotional experience for many individuals. However, the concept needs to be differentiated from infant-stage symbiosis, Margaret Mahler's second stage of normal development. The latter idea, according to Pine (2004), was of “central” importance to Mahler. Although Pine acknowledges the influence of research findings and some critical challenges to this idea, he maintains nonetheless that it “remains quite valuable” and reports what he suggests are manifestations of symbiotic experiences in the infant-mother relationship. His recognition of infant observational data has led him to feature the idea of “moments” of merger between mother and infant. Empirical data, however, challenge both the idea of a more broadly defined stage of infantile symbiosis and the more narrowly conceived “moments.” A different understanding of development in the presence of early-stage symbiosis is provided.

Do We Do What We Think We Do? Implicit Theories in the Analyst's Mind

Matthew Silvan

Matthew Silvan

149 West 93rd Street
New York, NY 10025
E-mail: mes57@columbia.edu

Matthew Silvan  

Irene Cairo introduced the panel by noting that psychoanalysis has always been fundamentally interested in the process of self-knowledge, both our patients' and our own. However, the means of undertaking this process has always been our own minds, which are “admittedly imperfect and inevitably limited.” Thus, our instrument in this epistemological journey distorts and can even cripple the analytic endeavor. From this perspective it becomes essential to better understand what shapes and influences our observations. In the past century, numerous theories have been developed to help us study and explain this process. These theories themselves shape our observations, influence our technique, and inform our interpretations. Some are public, conscious, and more clearly at the forefront of our thinking. Others may exist in the back of our minds, to become explicit only when we look back at what we have actually thought or said. These other ideas, experiences, theories, models, and concepts, perhaps less articulated, and likely less conscious, exist in our minds and impact our work with no less power. Joseph Sandler has referred to them as “implicit theories.” This panel, Cairo noted, would address “the question of whether or not there is a correspondence between our ideas about the analytic theory on which we base our practice and the actual clinical practice that we apply.” It would not concern itself with trying to determine which if any theory is right.

Panel held at the Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 23, 2004.

Panelists: Irene Cairo (chair), Jorge Canestri, Peter Fonagy, Dorothy Holmes.

945

[[RunningHead]]

Constructive Dialogue and Editorial Policy

Neil Altman and Jody Davies

Jody Davies

127 West 79th Street #3
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: neilaltman@hotmail.com

441 West End Avenue #2C
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: daviesjm@aol.com

Jody Davies  

January 25, 2004. In response to Eagle, Wakefield, and Wolitzky's allegation in JAPA51/Supplement (pp. 164, 178) that in our article in that issue we advocate “censorship,” we wish to make clear that we advocate nothing of the sort. Our point was that we believe the editors of psychoanalytic journals have a responsibility to promote constructive dialogue by limiting unnecessary contentiousness and doing their best to ensure that articles they publish fairly represent the work of authors being critiqued.

As editors of Psychoanalytic Dialogues we have on a number of occasions asked authors to revise articles when we thought they did not represent the views of other authors accurately or fairly or when the tone of those articles seemed disrespectful to the authors whose work was being critiqued. This in no way interferes with the peer review process, and it is not necessary to reject a paper that has been recommended for acceptance. One need only ask the author to make the necessary changes in tone or emphasis prior to publication. Of course, there is often plenty of room for disagreement in any specific case as to whether an author's views are being fairly represented, and we do believe in keeping our conversations lively, so we do not expect to agree in all specific instances. Nonetheless, we believe that one of the primary responsibilities of an editor is to create the conditions in which productive exchange can take place. This was, after all, the topic we were invited to address in our articles. Our concern was with creating a tone of mutual respect and intellectual excellence, not with censorship.

267

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Neuroscience

Wider than the Sky: The Phenomenal Gift of Consciousness. By Gerald M. Edelman. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2004, 201 pp., $24.00.

Arnold Modell

Arnold Modell

82 Kirkstall Road
Newtonville, MA 02460
E-mail: amodell617@aol.com

Arnold Modell   Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School.

Why should psychoanalysts, who are preeminently students of the unconscious, be interested in the problem of consciousness? Two answers suggest themselves. First, one cannot understand the unconscious mind without having consciousness as a point of reference. One cannot be an atheist if there are no true believers. Second, consciousness and the perception of the self are nearly coterminous. Integration and differentiation characterize both consciousness and self. Emotions and feelings, derived from what Edelman calls value systems, are intrinsic to the evolution of consciousness and, as we know, are salient regarding the development of the self. These are very good reasons why psychoanalysts should be interested in the subject of consciousness.

Gerald Edelman, who received the Nobel Prize for contributions to immunology, began his investigations of the brain over twenty-five years ago. He accepted two ultimate challenges facing neuroscience. The first was to identify the neural correlates of consciousness; the second, to explain how the “feel”of subjective experience can emerge from chemical and electrical processes constrained by the laws of the physical world. How can matter become imagination? Explaining this “feel,” or the quality of subjective experience, is customarily referred to as the problem of qualia, a problem that has bugged philosophers for centuries. Some claim that mind/brain transformations are of such an order of complexity as to preclude a solution, that the human mind is incapable of framing an explanation, that a true understanding of the neural origin of subjectivity lies beyond our cognitive abilities. Edelman forcibly rejects this position, as Wider Than the Sky attempts to solve the mystery of qualia and to describe the neural correlates of consciousness.

625

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Research

Subliminal Explorations of Perception, Dreams, and Fantasies: The Pioneering Contributions of Charles Fisher. Edited by Howard Shevrin. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 2003, 197 pp., $49.95.

Steven Ellman

Steven Ellman

140 Riverside Drive
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: selman174@aol.com

Steven Ellman   Professor Emeritus, CUNY Graduate Center; President and Training Analyst, IPTAR; Supervisor, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

Subliminal Explorations of Perception Dreams, and Fantasies details Charles Fisher's seminal contributions while serving as wonderful reminder of the intellectual ferment of the late 1940s on to the 1970s. Since understanding this era is important in contextualizing Fisher's contributions, let me spend a moment describing a major figure of that era. Fisher was a fellow traveler to a group of psychoanalysts who were students and colleagues of David Rapaport. The group included Merton Gill, George Klein, Roy Schafer, and Robert Holt, to name but a few. Rapaport's genius, largely unappreciated today, stimulated research in such diverse areas as states of consciousness and learning research (1967). He attempted to show that a psychoanalytic model of learning could make predictions and explain phenomena that eluded traditional learning theories. Unfortunately, contemporary analysts have somehow misplaced his empirical contributions. His students, however, became strongly involved in subliminal research and the study of states of consciousness, and developed new areas of inquiry, such as research in cognitive styles. Rapaport, of course, is remembered for his pioneering attempts to systematize Freudian theory. His theoretical formulations have been somewhat discounted since critics often confuse his psychoanalytic language (unfortunately replete with allusions to psychic energy) with his elegant conceptualizations. Charles Fisher's research in subliminal research might have suffered a similar fate, but fortunately Howard Shevrin, the editor of this volume, has brought together Fisher's contributions in this area. Shevrin's introduction demonstrates how Fisher presages a good deal of the current research being done by cognitive psychologists.

Child Psychoanalysis and Obsessive-Compulsive Symptoms

The Treatment of a Ten-Year-Old Boy

Rex McGehee

Rex McGehee

390 Harrison Street
Denver, CO 80206
Fax: 303-322-3394
E-mail: rnmcgehee@earthlink.net

Rex McGehee   Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of Colorado HealthSciences Center; Training and Supervising Analyst, Denver Institute for Psychoanalysis.

Obsessive-compulsive symptoms have long held interest for psychoanalysts. Currently, pharmacological and behavioral treatments are commonly viewed as the standard of care, and psychoanalytic clinicians are often uncertain of how best to approach these patients. The successful two-year psychoanalytic treatment of a ten-year-old boy with obsessive-compulsive disorder, who remained symptom-free eight years after termination, is reported. While this is but a single case, its outcome implies that for some patients psychoanalysis may be the treatment of choice.

[[RunningHead]]

History and Psychoanalysis

Psychoanalysis and History (The Annual of Psychoanalysis, Vol. XXXI). Edited by Jerome A. Winer and James William Anderson for the Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis and the Chicago Psychoanalytic Society. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 2003, 272 pp., $49.90.

Hilary Beattie

Hilary Beattie

15A East 10th Street (1B)
New York, NY 10003
E-mail: hjb1@columbia.edu

Hilary Beattie  

Almost one hundred years ago Freud (1910), in his “Leonardo da Vinci and a Memory of His Childhood,” pioneered the application of psychoanalytic ideas to the understanding of historical personages and events, and thereby set in motion a series of controversies that to some degree still surround the whole idea of “psychohistory.” Half a century later William L. Langer, in his famous 1958 presidential address to the American Historical Association, lamented that little ef fective use had so far been made of psychoanalysis by historians, and declared that this should therefore be their “next assignment.” The laudable purpose of the present volume of essays by a diverse group of authors, drawn from disciplines including psychoanalysis, psychology, history, political science, and international studies, is to assess the status of the use of psychoanalysis in history today, almost another half-century on.

These eighteen relatively brief papers cover a broad range of topics, loosely grouped under six headings: the case for psychobiography; Freud and beyond (covering Freud's own work in the genre and the potential uses of the theories of his followers); the author's journey (on the writer's emotional involvement with his or her topic); psychoanalytic approaches to the study of American presidents; psychoanalytic perspectives on religious history; and psychoanalytic history and culture. Inevitably, the essays are somewhat uneven in quality. They also represent only a sampling of what may currently be going on in the field, rather than offering a coherent perspective on the history of psychohistory itself, something that Bruce Mazlish, in the concluding paper, “The Past and Future of Psychohistory,” admits still remains to be written (p. 252). For a sympathetic and lucid overview of the impact of psychoanalysis on historical studies as a whole, and the resistances it has engendered, the reader would do better to turn to Freud for Historians by Peter Gay (1985), himself one of the undisputed masters of the genre. The opposing case was perhaps best put, in highly tendentious style, by David E. Stannard in Shrinking History: On Freud and the Failure of Psychohistory (1980).

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Research

Experiences of Depression: Theoretical, Clinical, and Research Perspectives. By Sidney J. Blatt. Washington, DC: American Psychological Association, 2004, xiii + 359 pp., $49.45.

Robert Wallerstein

Robert Wallerstein

290 Beach Road
Belvedere, CA 94920
Fax: 415-789-9530
E-mail: judywall@mindspring.co

Robert Wallerstein   Emeritus Professor, Department of Psychiatry, University of California at San Francisco, School of Medicine; Emeritus Training and Supervising Analyst, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute.

Just as physical pain is the most common somatic symptom propelling people to seek medical care, so are depressive symptoms or depressive character dispositions (allied often enough with anxiety and dissatisfaction with work performance and relationship patterns) the most common complaint of those coming into the mental health care system: reason enough to welcome this magisterial study by Sidney Blatt and his gifted colleagues into the psychopathology and psychotherapy of the depressive experience. Blatt, a psychoanalyst and empirical psychological researcher, is properly praised, in Peter Fonagy's foreword to the book, as “one of the giants in our field.” This volume is the culmination of an extremely productive life's work, one joining clinical study to empirical research into the nature of the depressive experience. This should not be read, of course, as noting the end of this life's work; I trust it will long continue, on the same wideningly generative path.

What is the essence of Blatt's impressive exposition here? It is simply this: that clinical depression and/or the depressive character formation are not unitary clinical entities; rather, Blatt, based on his clinical psychoanalytic experience and thirty years of empirical research, has distinguished two fundamentally different depressive diatheses, designated anaclitic and introjective, differing in symptomatology and character attributes, and differing as well in the formative childhood experiences that create a predisposition to the one or the other, and in implications for prognosis and psychotherapeutic outcome.

Anaclitic (or dependent) depression is marked by interpersonal issues of care, connectedness, and relatedness. A central fear is of abandonment, loss, and helplessness, to use the Engel-Schmale idiom (Engel and Reichsman 1956; Schmale 1972). Here we see a desperate need to maintain supporting and nurturant links, and to ward off feelings of being unwanted and unloved. At its life-threatening extremes in infancy and toddlerhood, it can be manifest as hospitalism and marasmus, as chronicled by Spitz (1965; Spitz and Wolf 1946) and the Robertsons (Robertson and Robertson 1971). It occurs more frequently in females, who according to Blatt are more oriented to issues of relatedness than are males.

Morris Eagle  

Previous research suggests a relation between attachment style and the degree of satisfaction one experiences in a romantic relationship (Feeney 2002; Keelan, Dion, and Dion 1998; Meyers and Landsberger 2002). However, few studies have examined how attachment is related to specific features of relational functioning. This study examined the relationship history of both single and married men and women, to discern which aspects of this history and which dimensions (anxiety/avoidance) and attachment style categories (secure, enmeshed-preoccupied, avoidant-dismissive, avoidant-fearful) predict cohesiveness, consensus, satisfaction, affectional expression, and overall adjustment in one's romantic relationship.

Method

An ethnically diverse sample of 90 undergraduate and graduate students from Adelphi University, who had ever been or were at the time of the study involved in a monogamous romantic relationship of at least three months’ duration were studied. The 33 men and 57 women in the sample ranged in age from 17 to 50 (Mean = 22.96, SD = 7.23); 80% were Caucasian, 3.3% African American, 3.3% Hispanic, 1.19% Native American, 6.7% Asian, and 6.7% other.

Participants were asked to complete a measure of attachment, Experiences in Close Relationships-Revised (Brennan, Clark, and Shaver 1996), and a measure of relational functioning, the Dyadic Adjustment Scale, or DAS (Spanier 1976). Additionally, participants completed a series of questions concerning a particular past or current relationship, the historical status of their relationship (whether it is past or current), marital status, whether the relationship had ever been broken off and then resumed, the number of breakups that had occurred over the course of the relationship, the number of past serious relationships in which the individual had been involved, and the length of the relationship.

Findings

Four demographic variables (age, marital status, status of the relationship, and number of breakups) were found to be significantly related to at least one of the five measures of relational functioning.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Developmental Theory

Psychoanalytic Theories: Perspectives From Developmental Psychopathology. By Peter Fonagy and Mary Target. New York: Brunner-Routledge, 2003, 417 pp., $37.95.

Michael Shulman

Michael Shulman

117 North First Street, Suite 113
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
E-mail: mshulmanphd@lycos.com

Michael Shulman   Adjunct faculties, University of Michigan, University of Toledo, Madonna University; Advanced Candidate, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute.

Opposition is True Friendship.

—WILLIAM BLAKE

At any time psychoanalytic theory is like a family of ideas, with resemblances, relationships and feuds, and with new members expected to take their place alongside the rest and respect the authority of the ancestors, especially Freud. If a psychoanalytic approach is to survive,

1388

there must be ways of pruning this “family tree,” choosing between rival explanations so that the body of theory is strengthened rather than continuing to branch out [Fonagy and Target, p. 289].

This book by Peter Fonagy and Mary Target is a major text for psychoanalysis. But it is a “stealth” work: masquerading as a mild-mannered review of developmental research that bears on psychoanalytic theories, this book is not only a comprehensive contemporary synthesis of developmental theory and research in psychoanalysis, but also a full-scale presentation and critique of major ideas in the evolution of psychoanalytic theories of development and psychopathology. It is also, and most fascinatingly to my mind, an examination of psychoanalytic ways of theorizing and of some of their unintended consequences. It is at once systematic and incisive in its argument—a Fenichel for the twenty-first century, in disguise.

Like Fenichel's 1945 work, which was as close to a comprehensive synthesis of psychoanalytic theory of psychopathology as has ever been of fered, Fonagy and Target's book is a monument to scholarship and close reading. Psychoanalytic Theories: Perspectives from Developmental Psychopathology features a 75-page bibliography, which I would estimate contains over 1,500 references to works of analytic theorists and researchers (Fenichel's had a similar number of citations). The authors cited include North American and British theorists familiar to most American analysts, but psychoanalytic voyagers Fonagy and Target have journeyed not only between London and New York, but also between Scotland and Paris (though the visit is limited primarily to time spent with André Green and in the company of a few ideas of Lacan), Budapest, and Buenos Aires.

responseSet.title: The Child Analyst at Work
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Child Analyst at Work

Phyllis Tyson

Phyllis Tyson

Phyllis Tyson  

A developmental point of view has been central to psychoanalysis from the time Freud embarked on his own self-analysis. Almost as old as this developmental perspective are debate and skepticism about the merits and liabilities of using the findings of developmental observational research or child analysis to inform or modify psychoanalytic theory. There is even skepticism about whether the very practice of child analysis is properly psychoanalytic. Nevertheless, both child analysis and the developmental perspective have remained at the psychoanalytic table. Now, in recognition of its place and importance in psychoanalysis, a regular section on child analysis and development will be featured at least once a year in JAPA. We invite your suggestions, submissions, commentaries, and questions.

The papers in this section attest the continuing survival and value of child analysis. They also speak to a continuing contribution that child analysts can make to the broader field of mental health. By sharing our experiences, findings, and insights we might challenge some of the current assumptions being made that influence treatment guidelines. The papers are clinical, and address various aspects of the child analyst at work. Interestingly, although each deals with clinical problems and the child analyst's approach to these problems, each paper, in one way or another, touches on the special clinical challenges that arise in the face of a child's intense affect, angry feelings in particular. These challenges are technical on one hand, related to the way the analyst manages the child's feelings and the behavior used to express them in the session. On the other hand, they are emotional. How does the analyst deal with the intense countertransference feelings that these children can elicit? Each paper demonstrates the enormous therapeutic benefit that can be gained when a child has the opportunity to express, verbalize, and understand intense feelings within the safety of an analytic situation. Although the patients in these papers may be children, the papers are of value to all analysts faced with challenging, emotion-laden behaviors from their patients.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Shame Conflicts and Tragedy in The Scarlet Letter

Benjamin Kilborne

Benjamin Kilborne

5 Lenox Road
West Stockbridge, MA 01266
E-mail: bkilborne@aol.com

Benjamin Kilborne   Corresponding member, Los Angeles Institute and Society for Psychoanalytic Studies and Los Angeles Psychoanalytic Society and Institute.

Nathaniel Hawthorne's The Scarlet Letter has much to teach psychoanalysts and psychotherapists. Perhaps no other American novel lends itself so well to an exploration in depth of the dynamics, conflicts, and defenses characteristic of shame. While most commentators on The Scarlet Letter have assumed Hester Prynne's pain to be shame-based, and the Reverend Dimmesdale's to be guilt-based, a rather different interpretation is proposed namely, that both are afflicted with shame, but that Dimmesdale's is more unbearable than Hester's because more conflictual, less representable, and less easily used protectively. Dimmesdale's shame is at once deeper and more toxic. What “deeper” and “more toxic” mean in the context of shame conflicts (including conflicts to which feelings of shame give rise) is explored.

Wild Analysis: A New Freud Translation

Wild Analysis. By Sigmund Freud. Translated by Alan Bance with an introduction by Adam Phillips. London: Penguin Books, 2002, xxxii + 222 pp., $20.01.

Peter Loewenberg

Peter Loewenberg

449 Levering Avenue
Los Angeles, CA 90024-1909
Fax: 310-471-0048
E-mail: ibenaub@mvs.oac.ucla.edu

Peter Loewenberg   Co-dean and Co-chair of the Education Committee, New Center for Psychoanalysis; Professor of History and Political Psychology, UCLA.

Den eben wo Begriffe fehlen

Da stellt ein Wort zur rechten Zeit sich ein.

[Precisely where concepts fail you,

A timely word will come to mind.]

—Goethe, Faust I:1995-1996

A new translation of Freud is a significant event because all translation is also an interpretation, a rewriting, and inevitably, a betrayal. Due to the inherently unsatisfactory nature of translation, when it matters, many of us control the English translations of Freud against the German original. Now we have a new resource to use and play against, an accessible and vernacular postmodern Freud, under the general editorship of Adam Phillips, who does not know German. Fifteen volumes are planned; twelve have already been published. Phillips rejects chronology for this series, proposing instead a thematic organization. Each volume has a different translator, thus sacrificing the internal consistency, excellent cross-referencing, and the more or less uniform lexical use of language and style of the Strachey edition. The introductory essays are all by nonpsychoanalysts—scholars of literature, the history of science, and philosophy. The issue of Freud's language is pivotal because his ideas have shaped our twenty-first-century perception of our world and of our humanity. His terms of reference are now colloquial in the media and in our daily speech. How Freud is translated determines how he is read and how his ideas are applied clinically and in the culture. A colleague who was active in founding a new psychoanalytic institute once argued: “Who needs to read Freud on technique? Physicists don't read Einstein. Read the new stuff!

[[RunningHead]]

Abstracts of the 2005 Poster Session of the American Psychoanalytic Association Winter Meeting

Changes in Object Relations Following Intensive Psychoanalytically Oriented Inpatient Treatment

John Porcerelli , Golan Shahar , Sidney Blatt , Richard Ford , Jacqueline Mezza and Lisa Greenlee

Lisa Greenlee

Department of Family Medicine
Wayne State University School of Medicine
15400 West McNichols, 2nd Floor
Detroit, MI 48235
E-mail: jporcer@med.wayne.edu

Lisa Greenlee  

Considerable advances have been made in recent years in the assessment of mental representations. One of the most reliable and valid measures of mental representations is the Social Cognition and Object Relations Scale (Westen et al. 1990). The present study used data from the Riggs-Yale Project (Blatt and Ford 1994) to assess changes in mental representations following intensive inpatient psychoanalytically oriented treatment of severely disturbed, treatment-resistant patients.

Participants and Procedures

The study included 84 patients (mean age = 21). Patients received, on average, 1.5 years of psychoanalytically oriented treatment and had undergone psychological testing at admission and at the end of the study period. Most patients were at least middle-class, with at least average IQs. Approximately 30% were diagnosed with a DSM-III psychotic condition. Object relations were coded from six TAT cards (1, 5, 12 M, 13 MF, 14, 15).

The Social Cognition and Object Relations Scale (SCORS) includes four dimensions of object relations, each scored on a 5-point scale with scores of 5 being healthy. Complexity of Representations (CR) assesses degree of differentiation, integration and complexity. Affect-tone of Relationships (AT) assesses malevolence (vs. benevolence) of relationships. Capacity for Emotional Investment (EI) assesses the degree of need-gratifying vs. mutual relatedness. Understanding Social Causality (SC) assesses the degree to which social attributions are logical, accurate, and psychologically minded.

Findings

Interrater reliability using intraclass correlations (Shrout and Fleiss 1979) of the SCORS ranged from.73 to.84.

1323

[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Theory and Practice

Harvesting Free Association. By Marita Torsti-Hagman; translated by Ellen Valle and Kristina Jalas. London: Free Association Books, 2003, 174 pp., $29.95.

Leon Hoffman

Leon Hoffman

167 East 67th Street
New York, NY 10021
Fax: 212-327-0640
E-mail: 73542.334@compsuerve.com

Leon Hoffman   Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Institute; Codirector, Pacella Parent Child Center, New York Psychoanalytic Society.

It is always valuable to read a book that is not consonant with one's way of conceptualizing psychoanalytic material. In fact, learning about a different point of view can stimulate a reader's ability to critically approach the strengths and weaknesses of his or her own perspective. In this case, the book under review has propelled me to research further the concepts of “inner space” and “inner genital.”

In this volume, Marita Torsti-Hagman, a Finnish psychoanalyst, presents a series of papers, most previously published. Focusing on the fundamentals of free association, for both patient and analyst, she writes about hysteria, obsessional neurosis, and the death drive. However, the one overriding theme of the book is the role of inner genitality in feminine psychology.

To understand Torsti-Hagman's general psychoanalytic perspective, one should peruse the three chapters in the middle of the book: “Quo Vadis Psychoanalysis?” “A Psychoanalysis Which Seeks Popularity Is a Lost Psychoanalysis,” and “André Green and the Dead Mother.” She argues that “the emphasis on a ‘scientific’ perspective … may lead to the loss of the very heart of psychoanalysis” (p. 71) because the scientific “model is totally inappropriate to the analytical way of thinking” (p. 78). Using André Green as her model (her ego ideal, I would say), Torsti-Hagman maintains that “psychoanalytic theory unfolds neither through logical understanding nor study, but through reading with our analytic tool, free association, through understanding and through interpreting our reading experience” (p. 80).

Overall, I wonder whether this approach leads Torsti-Hagman to assume too often that her conjectures and inferences about a patient's unconscious mental life are veridical. This is my main critique of the volume's perspective.

1018

Psychoanalysis and Neuroscience

Imagination and the Meaningful Brain. By Arnold H. Modell. Cambridge: MIT Press, 2003, 271 pp., $35.00.

Leon Hoffman

Leon Hoffman

167 East 67th Stree
New York, NY 10021
Fax: 212-327-0640
E-mail: 73542.334@compuserve.co

Leon Hoffman   Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Institute; Codirector, Pacella Parent Child Center, New York Psychoanalytic Society.

In this deceptively small book Arnold Modell demonstrates for us his erudition and wide-ranging knowledge of the classics, philosophy, philosophy of science, cognitive psychology, neuroscience, art, music, and of course psychoanalysis. I can touch here on only a few of the issues he raises.

Modell sets for himself an enormous task. In fact, his final paragraph can be read as a challenge to the rest of us to replicate and extend what he attempts in this volume: “As a final observation on the mind-body problem, I turn again to the moderating voice of the philosopher Thomas Nagel. The mind-body problem is, in his opinion, still unsolved. What is required for its solution, he suggests, is a radical revision in our concept of the mind, a radical alteration in how we conceptualize mind. It requires an unimagined expansion of the concept of mind that would simultaneously include neural events. Mental states have a ‘tripartite essence—phenomenological, functional, and physiological.’ It is an illusion to conceive of these aspects separately” (p. 203). Unquestionably, Modells's work is an attempt to realize that vision.

636

responseSet.title: A Developmental Perspective on the Ideal of Reason in American Constitutional Law
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Developmental Perspective on the Ideal of Reason in American Constitutional Law

Anne Dailey

Anne Dailey

65 Elizabeth Street
Hartford, CT 06105
E-mail: adailey@law.uconn.edu

Anne Dailey   Evangeline Starr Professor of Law, University of Connecticut School of Law.

The ideal of reason is central to contemporary accounts of citizenship in American constitutional law. The individual capacity for reasoned choice lies closely aligned with the constitutional values of personal liberty and democratic self-government as they have evolved in Supreme Court decisions over the past century. Yet as presently conceived, the ideal of reason in constitutional law overlooks the process by which individuals actually acquire the capacity to choose their own values and commitments or to engage in reasoned thinking about collective ends. This paper argues that we cannot hope to sustain and foster a constitutional polity committed to the principles of individual liberty and democratic self-government without knowing something about how individual citizens come to possess this requisite skill of mind. A developmental perspective on reason in constitutional law provides a framework for examining the source and contours of the psychological skills that make it possible to lead an autonomous, self-directed life and to participate meaningfully in the processes of democratic self-government. Developmental psychology, together with research in related fields, provides empirical support for the proposition that the psychological capacity for reasoned thinking has its roots in the early caregiving relationship. Thus, a comprehensive and integrated constitutional family law must recognize the role of early caregiving in the political socialization of children. This developmental approach offers a substantial reworking of constitutional doctrine in the areas of family privacy, parental rights, congressional power, and affirmative welfare rights.

[[RunningHead]]

A New Look at Core Gender and Gender Role Identity in Women: Commentary

Catharine Stimpson

Catharine Stimpson

New York, NY 10003
E-mail: catharine.stimpson@nyu.edu

Catharine Stimpson   Dean of the Graduate School of Arts and Science and University Professor, New York University.

Nancy J. Chodorow, Helen K. Gediman, and Adrienne Harris have written lucidly and instructively about the connections among psychoanalysis, feminism, and their own lives.1 As an outsider to psychoanalysis, I have learned from their papers and admire their scope and sincerity. Like all serious work, they provide rich insights but raise questions as well. The ones I wish to explore are periodization, the use of the term postmodernism, and the persistence of patriarchies and neopatriarchies. I tie my explorations together in an unexpected case study: a visit to Wichita, Kansas.

Periodization

One of the most important tasks of historians, periodization is the naming of epochs and ages. It was a great concern of the first generation of feminist historians, who believed that the reigning periodization of Western history distorted the experiences of women. Famously, Joan Kelly asked, “Did women have a Renaissance?” and answered her question in the negative. In “The Social Relations of the Sexes,” Kelly (1976)

Submitted for publication May 25, 2005.

1Full disclosure: I know both Nancy J. Chodorow and Adrienne Harris, though we see each other infrequently. The NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, in which Helen K. Gediman is a clinical professor, is a unit of the NYU Graduate School of Arts and Science, of which I am dean.1119

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

In Praise of Simplicity: Commentary on Ablon and Jones

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy

Gower Street
London WC1E 6BT
E-mail: p.fonagy@ucl.ac.uk

Peter Fonagy   Freud Memorial Professor of Psychoanalysis, University College London;Chief Executive, The Anna Freud Centre, London.

Stuart Ablon and his mentor, the late Enrico Jones, have made a major contribution to psychotherapy research in pioneering a standardized coding system for therapeutic interaction that has enabled workers to extract from the almost infinite complexity of the interpersonal interchange between therapist and patient those indicators which are most likely to be theory-driven and to relate to therapeutic outcome.

The Q-set instrument they have devised offers a particular level of analysis for exploring the process of psychological therapy. The Psychotherapy Process Q-set (PQS) represents a specific setting on the psychotherapy researcher's microscope that brings into relief particular features of the therapeutic process while necessarily blurring others. No valid and reliable measurement approach in psychotherapy research is capable of keeping all levels of analysis of a treatment in focus simultaneously. The clinician's belief that this is desirable and may be possible through active participation of the observer in the therapeutic process is an illusion, and a somewhat self-serving one at that. As participants, either as patients or as analysts, our focus rapidly shifts between different levels of analysis. At one moment we choose to focus on microanalytic aspects of the interchange, at another overarching thematic features that may define an entire analytic treatment. In clinical reports our focus may be sentences exchanged that hint at transference themes or at life experiences brought from outside the session. As clinicians we allow our minds to scan the entire scene, our eyes moving from features close to us to other features almost merging with the distant horizon. Yet just like the person scanning a landscape we experience everything as in focus at the same time.

579

2983, avenue de Soissons

CANADA

Dominique Scarfone  

A Portuguese proverb, I believe, defines universality as the local thing minus the surrounding walls. Psychoanalysis, then, is a potentially good provider of universal knowledge, founded as it is on the local, even intimate endeavor. The problem is how to get rid of the surrounding walls—without destroying, that is, the whole enterprise in the process.

From Late Adolescence To Young Adulthood. By David Dean Brockman Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 2003, 307 pp., $39.95.

Charles Parks

Charles Parks

6505 Wiscasset Road
Bethesda, MD 20816
Fax: 301-320-7299
E-mail: ceparks@speakeasy.net

Charles Parks  

For David Dean Brockman, the processes of personality consolidation that occur during young adulthood make this period of life “the most exciting of all phases of development…. Physical maturation has been completed. The monumental developmental tasks of integration, synthesis, and cohesion of multiple areas of personality result in a ‘strikingly observable settling down’ into a chosen career, searching for and finding a suitable partner with whom to achieve physical and emotional intimacy, mastery of the drives, fitting in with a social group identity, and the consequent establishment of clearly def ined sexual and gender identities” (p. 257). Three chapters of Brockman's book are devoted to the psychoanalytic assessment of young adults and an analytic exploration of two concepts related to forming the young adult personality: identity, and gender and sexual identity. The final four chapters discuss specif ic forms of developmental breakdown in young adulthood.

296

Lacan

The Cambridge Companion to Lacan. Edited by Jean-Michel Rabaté. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2003, 287 pp., $23.99.

Dominique Scarfone

Dominique Scarfone

2983 avenue de Soissons
Montréal, QC H3S 1W1
CANADA
E-mail: dominique.scarfone@umontreal.ca

Dominique Scarfone   Training and Supervising Analyst, Canadian Psychoanalytic Institute; Professor, Department of Psychology, University of Montreal.

The New Orleans Congress of the International Psychoanalytical Association, held in March 2004, marked a turning point. For the first time since the early sixties, when Lacan left the IPA (or, as his followers prefer to say, was excommunicated from it), a number of Lacanian analysts were invited to one of its major panels. This is no chance event and likely reflects a number of changes that have occurred both within the IPA and within the Lacanian movement itself. After Lacan's death in 1981, this movement has undergone a series of splits, making for a galaxy of groups, circles, societies, and schools. Mind you, except for the severing of official organizational links, this is not unlike the birth of the many “currents” and “schools of thought” that work, rather independently, under the IPA's banner. Hence, the differentiation happening both within the IPA and among Lacanians is probably one reason why at least some representatives of French Lacanianism were considered appropriate interlocutors by the IPA leadership, with personal acquaintance among the people concerned doing the rest. This is also, in my view, a natural extension of the inescapable fact that Lacan's theory has gained a significant audience around the world, including the U.S., where many analysts in “mainstream” organizations such as APsA demonstrate at least some curiosity about this once abhorred brand of Freudianism.

I have suggested a possible parallel between the IPA and the Lacanian movement.

997

Relational Child Psychotherapy. By Neil Altman, Richard Briggs, Jay Frankel, Daniel Gensler, and Pasqual Pantone. New York: Other Press, 2002, 410 pp., $50.00.

Joanne Naegele

Joanne Naegele

12429 Cedar Road, Suite 12
Cleveland Heights, OH 44106
Phone: 216-791-2711
E-mail: jnaegele@aol.com

Joanne Naegele   Chair of Subcommittee on Child Analysis, Cleveland Psychoanalytic Center; faculty, Hanna Perkins Center.

Five professionals connected in various ways with the William Alanson White Institute have produced this book. Two are Neil Altman and Jay Frankel, editors of Psychoanalytic Dialogues: A Journal of Relational Perspectives. Their purpose is to study child therapy from an interpersonal and relational psychoanalytic perspective. Their book attempts to conceptualize that treatment. It succeeds, it seems to me, not only in presenting that point of view but in being helpful to any child psychoanalytic psychotherapist.

Written for those working with children up to around the beginning of puberty, the book describes effectively the complexity of the child's world. Chapter 11, one of the best clinically relevant chapters of the book, is titled “Launching the Therapy with the Child.” The authors quote E. Loewald (1987)

[[RunningHead]]

Abstracts of the 2005 Poster Session of the American Psychoanalytic Association Winter Meeting

The use of Foucault's Historical Ontology to Research Key Concepts in the History of Psychoanalysis

Robin Ward

Robin Ward

One University Place
Chester, PA 19013
E-mail: robinward@hotmail.com

Robin Ward  

The French philosopher and historian Michel Foucault is among the most recognizable of twentieth-century intellectuals. He is most well known for his work on the history of mental illness as well as for his explorations into the birth of the modern prison system. Foucault (1984) is less well known for the development of the method by which he engaged in these various projects: namely, his historical ontology, a research method, both archeological and genealogical in nature, whereby he examined discrete instances of historical discourse on the topics of his interest with the intent of documenting when and where specific linguistic practices came into being and the manner in which they changed.

My assertion is that Foucault's historical ontological method provides a useful tool for examining concepts of historical relevance to psychoanalysis, of fering a framework for the rigorous examination of the historical contexts wherein key notions of psychoanalysis came into being, while also documenting the manner in which the meaning of these discursive practices changed. Engaging in such an endeavor provides a unique opportunity to gain new appreciations of the history of our field, as well as the meaning of our contemporary practice. By way of example, consider neurasthenia, a diagnosis of singular importance in the history of psychoanalysis.

My current project involves using Foucault's historical ontology as a method of exploring the historical discourse surrounding neurasthenia. In most histories of psychiatry (e.g., Kaplan and Sadock 1998), first use of the diagnosis of neurasthenia is attributed to the prominent New York psychiatrist George Miller Beard. Beard did indeed publish a great deal on the diagnosis (e.g., Beard 1881). However, there is indication that several months before Beard is reputed to have introduced the term, it had already been articulated by Edwin Van Deusen, a physician from Kalamazoo, Michigan (Bynum 2003).

How is it that neurasthenia emerged in this particular cultural and historical context? And how did the name Van Deusen come to be buried in the tomes of history? What was the economic and industrial climate? What was the role of the physician?

responseSet.title: The Legacy of Enrico Jones
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Legacy of Enrico Jones

Stuart Hauser

Stuart Hauser

Stuart Hauser  

In this first research section of 2005 we celebrate the stunning accomplishments and legacy of the late Enrico Jones, a major researcher in psychoanalysis and psychotherapy, especially in the area of psychoanalytic process, and a friend and colleague to countless students, researchers, and practitioners in those fields. Enrico's contributions were characterized by two features that he kept always in balance: his grasp of the depth and complexity of psychoanalytic ideas and therapeutic processes and a steadfast commitment to using appropriate empirical approaches to deepen their investigation. These concerns consistently informed his work and led to the many articles cited by J. Stuart Ablon in a paper coauthored with Jones, the target paper of this section. To complete the picture, four additional references (Jones 1995, 1997; Jones, Cumming, and Pulos 1993; Jones, and Price 1998) relevant to Jones's study of psychoanalytic processes are listed at the end of this introduction.

Our format for this section, the target paper by Ablon and Jones discussed at length by Peter Fonagy and Sidney Blatt, creates an especially felicitous “hall” in which to display key aspects and implications of Jones's thinking. Readers can “listen in” as two knowledgeable and seasoned psychoanalytic researchers engage the ideas offered by Ablon and Jones and can immerse themselves further as Ablon returns to the stage to reply to the appreciative, astute challenges raised by the discussants.

One of Enrico Jones's gifts to our field was to bring heightened awareness of the great relevance of an observational and data-analytic technique, the Q-sort, to psychoanalytic research. The Q-sort is an instrument used in virtually all of his clinical research. The approach appears yet again in Geoff Goodman's paper, the second contribution in this section. Despite Jones's influence, and recent Q-sort diagnosis applications by Drew Westen and colleagues (Westen and Arkowitz-Westen 1998; Westen and Shedler 1999; Shedler 2002), the technique is not on the radar screen of most psychoanalysts.

Warren Procci

Warren Procci

230 South San Rafael Avenue
Pasadena, CA 91105

Warren Procci   Treasurer, American Psychoanalytic Association; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, UCLA School of Medicine, Harbor/UCLA Medical Center, Torrance, CA.

Psychoanalysts don't always agree with one another on matters of theory or practice, but one issue on which there is strong consensus is the need to enhance our appeal to younger mental health professionals. This excellent, marvelously crafted book is an effort to succinctly articulate a psychodynamic approach to the treatment of serious depression. While the authors specif ically state that their book is not a manual, they do conceive of it as specifying a focused dynamic approach to depression that represents a step toward manualization. The audience most ideally suited for this work is indeed mental health clinicians beginning their careers.

[[RunningHead]]

Empirical Evidence Supporting the Conceptual Relatedness of Object Representations and Internal Working Models

Geoff Goodman

Geoff Goodman

720 Northern Boulevard
Brookville, NY 11548
Fax: 516-299-2738
E-mail: ggoodman@liu.edu

Geoff Goodman   Advanced psychoanalytic candidate, Psychoanalytic Training Institute of the New York Freudian Society

The proliferation of new developmental and clinical theories broadly termed “psychoanalytic” demands a methodology for making systematic comparisons to establish the commonalities and distinctions among them. This article presents an empirical method for the comparative evaluation of such theories. The Mother-To-Child Object Representation / Internal Working Model Q-sort is a 100-item instrument constructed to assess the quality of the prototypical mother's object representation and internal working model of her child at age five. Object relations judges were asked to sort these items for a complex, differentiated, integrated object representation of a five-year-old, attachment judges for a secure, coherent, freely valuing internal working model of a five-year-old. Judges' criterion Q-sorts in each group yielded a composite criterion Q-sort of the quality of the prototypical mother's mental representation of her child according to each group's theoretical perspective. The Spearman-Brown correlation between the two composite criterion Q-sorts was r = .90, p < .001, suggesting that when confined to a 100-item common vocabulary, judges representing each theoretical construct agreed on the conceptual relatedness of object representations and internal working models. The theoretical constructs of object representations and internal working models share common assumptions in need of further exploration.

The Evolution of Paternal Identity in Late Adulthood

Calvin Colarusso

Calvin Colarusso

1020 Prospect Street
Suite 415B
La Jolla, CA 92037
Fax: 858-454-2473
E-mail: calc@san.rr.com

Calvin Colarusso   Training and Supervising Analyst in Adult and Child Psychoanalysis, San DiegoPsychoanalytic Institute and Society; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of California at San Diego School of Medicine.

The experience of fatherhood in late adulthood has received little attention in the psychoanalytic literature. After a presentation of the literature on fatherhood and a consideration of the developmental tasks of late adulthood in which the experience of fatherhood is embedded, the dynamically charged, developmentally conflicted nature of the relationships between late-life fathers and their children is explored. A basic premise is that the changing nature of these relationships inevitably confronts the elderly patriarch with considerable intrapsychic turmoil, reflection, and conflict that must be engaged and mastered if these most significant relationships are to be maintained and sustained for both generations. Clinical examples illustrate the theoretical points presented.

Postanalytic Contacts in Institutes

Joseph Schachter

Joseph Schachter

160 West 66th Street
New York, NY 10023-6555
E-mail: jschachter2@nyc.rr.com

Joseph Schachter  

February 4, 2004. Howard Levine and Judith Yanof, members of the COPE Study Group on Boundary Violations, in the second paper from this group (“Boundaries and Postanalytic Contacts in Institutes,”JAPA52/3), focus on the possible ethical violations involved in post-termination contact after a training analysis.1 The authors’ consideration of possible ethical violations, unfortunately, is not embedded in the larger context of the complex clinical conceptions of the nature of post-termination contact. As a result, they fail to consider that their focused concern about possible ethical violations carries inappropriate, negative implications for the totality of clinical concerns in regard to such contact. Thus, their emphasis on a variety of serious risks implies that clinical participation in post-termination contact is a dangerous enterprise that may well place the analyst at risk for ethical violations and the patient at risk for exploitation. This implication, again, stems from their failure to consider the clinical nature and complexity of post-termination contact, including both the possibility that the absence of such contact may have deleterious effects on the former patient, analyst or nonanalyst, and that the presence of such contact may have substantial benefits.

The members of the study group, though geographically diverse, derive their psychoanalytic homogeneity from their selection by the chair of BOPS, which requires that they be certified and training analysts. One officer of BOPS commented that “comfortable” committees— shared views—are the most productive. The study group's shared, traditional point of view may additionally and inadvertently have been protected by the group process of avoiding, or at least not seeking, exposure to conflicting ideas. This conjecture is based on the fact that during the three years the study group was discussing post-termination contact (1996-1999), I conducted clinical discussion groups on that subject at each of the semiannual meetings of the American. To the best of my knowledge, none of the study group's sixteen members attended or participated in even one of those clinical discussion groups, which were accessible to them. In addition, they do not reference any of the four papers I wrote between 1990 and 1997 on post-termination contact.

Response to Doris Silverman

Fred Pine

Fred Pine

55 East 87th Street (1B)
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: fpine@att.net

Fred Pine   Emeritus Professor, Department of Psychiatry, Albert Einstein College ofMedicine; faculty, Columbia Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research; Adjunct Professor, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy.

Doris Silverman's critique of my recent paper reviewing and u pdating Mahler's concepts of symbiosis and separation-individuation (Pine 2004) is better directed at our original book (Mahler, Pine, and Bergman 1975), now thirty years old. Since the time of Emanuel Peterfreund's strong critique (1978), a work referred to by Silverman, I have written a series of increasingly clear and strong revisions of the ideas in that book (Pine 1981, 1990, 1992, 2004). Those writings are responsive to Silverman's entire present critique. In this note, I will assume the reader is familiar with Silverman's critique and not refer back to her paper point by point.

Silverman masses arguments and data regarding the infant's capacities for differentiation from birth onward and regarding aspects of subjective state (most notably, intentionality). I am quite familiar with that literature, regard it as invaluable, use it in my teaching, and have based theoretical writings on it (Pine 1990, 2005), writings that are central to my core views on psychoanalysis today. And I have clearly said, both in the paper Silverman critiques and elsewhere, that Mahler certainly “did not have it right” in her view of the fully undifferentiated state of the infant. (Mahler recognized this before her death as well, and was equally interested in the “competent infant” literature.)

Are ideas about merger, undifferentiatedness, boundarylessness— each a suggestive equivalent of “symbiosis,” to flesh out the term— still worth considering? My answer is yes, for three reasons. First, a full picture of mental life may require them. Second, ideas regarding merger experiences would help us understand one set

responseSet.title: Challenges that Probe our Premises
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Challenges that Probe our Premises

Lawrence Friedman

Lawrence Friedman

Lawrence Friedman  

No one will blame an analyst for feeling harassed by today's onslaught of controversies and theories. The profession has never been so multifariously unsettled. An unhappy state of affairs? On the contrary. These challenges are dragging every hidden assumption into the light, every taken-for-granted term, every unworked-out reference. Jostled by argument, routinely abbreviated formulas disgorge their full meaning, old promissory notes are discovered. We must spell out what we meant, recognize past choices, and judge new options. I think it's fair to say that contemporary psychoanalysis has unprecedented opportunities both to improve what needs improving and to understand better those aspects that endure.

Taking advantage of the opportunity, and hoping to use new ideas as a probe, JAPA will occasionally select a contribution that provokes special scrutiny of fundamental issues, and ask a few commentators not so much for a critique as for their own reflections on those issues.

What follows is a paper that may lead readers to think again about psychic causality and the determinateness of unconscious conflicts, or question their confidence that they control their spoken meanings, or weigh whether it is possible to bring psychodynamics and neurophysiology into harmony (the mind-body problem, again), or come to a new understanding of the “working alliance,” or reexamine the role of nonverbal treatment effects in the light of infant observation. Questions like these may come alive for the reader.

As for me, I start the paper marveling at how psychoanalysis capitalizes on the extraordinary creative power of ordinary speech. But then I find myself noting how the psychoanalytic situation complicates that ordinary phenomenon. And I finish by wondering exactly how that extra, analytic wrinkle manages to turn the everyday miracle of ordinary communication into something specifically therapeutic. With that question I sense that I have left the domain of the target paper, but not without gratitude to the authors for having prompted the journey.

[[RunningHead]]

Storytelling and Psychoanalytic Diversity

Joseph Schachter

Joseph Schachter

160 West 66th Street
New York, NY 10023-6555
E-mail: jschachter2@nyc.rr.com

Joseph Schachter  

November 16, 2003. Fred Busch opens his article “Telling Stories” (JAPA51/1) with the following statement: “The capacity of patients to tell and own their stories is central to their developing a sense of wellbeing from analysis” (p. 25). This assertion strikes me as reflecting Busch's belief and so requires qualification as his personal conviction, rather than as a recognized fact. Busch is certainly entitled to his personal conviction; indeed, it's questionable if an analyst could function clinically without one. However, in this age of psychoanalytic diversity there are many different beliefs about what is “central” to a patient's developing a sense of well-being from analysis, and we may assume that whatever is “central” probably varies with every patient-analyst dyad; one size does not fit all. I believe that there is widespread agreement about our uncertainty regarding the principal mutative factors in any individual's treatment.

Busch's report of case material documents a singular analytic approach. In his six-page summary of a session, there is no reference to the patient's current relationships or activities, either outside the consulting room or in relation to her analyst. He concludes: “I want to emphasize that this was one of those sessions in which a convergence of the patient's associations, feelings, and actions lends new clarity to the work” (p. 36). This conclusion does not address the patient's earlier protest “that it was her real life that she needed to be concerned with” (p. 30).

The patient's apparent improvement is irrelevant to the issue of the acknowledgment of contemporary analytic diversity. Analysts of many persuasions help patients by generating stories of quite different sorts, or by not generating stories at all. Accepting Busch's expression of an unqualified conviction that the analyst's therapeutic approach is “central,” and therefore the most effective form of treatment available, is inconsistent with that fact. We need to acknowledge the scientific view that in this age of psychoanalytic diversity we will not learn any time soon what is “central” to the most effective therapeutic approach.

[[RunningHead]]

The Struggle to Listen: Continuing Reflections, Lingering Paradoxes, and Some Thoughts on Recovery of Memory

Evelyne Schwaber

Evelyne Schwaber

4 Welland Road
Brookline, MA 02445
E-mail: Eschwaber@post.harvard.edu

Evelyne Schwaber  

This paper offers a further expansion of the author's continuing endeavor to highlight and explore subtle distinctions and lingering paradoxes in how we listen, and to reconsider their profound implications for clinical work and discovery. Several perhaps commonplace clinical moments are used to sharpen illumination on psychic experience that might otherwise remain outside conscious awareness, whether in the domain of the repressed or in other mnemonic realms, such as “implicit” or “procedural” memory. It is suggested that added dimensions of these different levels of memory may, through our struggle to listen, be ultimately knowable.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Capacity of Forgive: Intrapsychic and Developmental Perspectives

Leonard Horwitz

Leonard Horwitz

310 West 49th Street #1005
Kansas City, MO 64112
E-mail: lhorwitz@kc.rr.com

Leonard Horwitz   Training and Supervising Analyst, Greater Kansas City Psychoanalytic Institute.

The topic of forgiveness, despite its importance for the continuity of relationships and for the mental health of the aggrieved party, is relatively neglected in the psychoanalytic literature, perhaps because it is often seen as the province of religion and carries the connotation of reaction formation and inauthenticity. However, genuine forgiveness involves significant intrapsychic work, conscious and unconscious working through of one's anger, and putting the offense into the context of an integrated view of the whole person of the offender. Early developmental structures are the ground on which the relative ability to let go of a grievance depends. While later motives and defenses (e.g., fear of retraumatization, avoidance of shame) may also play a role, these early structures are primary. They are described here in terms of attainment of the depressive position and the development of a sense of secure attachment, the capacity to mentalize, and the ability to mourn.

[[RunningHead]]

The Social Face of Shame and Humiliation

Alisa Levine

Alisa Levine

137 Lake Avenue
Newton Centre, MA 02459
Fax: 617-630-8320
E-mail: alisarlevine@comcast.net

Alisa Levine  

This panel, chaired by Benjamin Kilborne, drew on psychoanalytic experiences and theories of shame and humiliation to address current social problems. When shame and humiliation are experienced as narcissistic injuries, they produce more shame. The injured individual wants to hide, feels helpless and enraged, and is preoccupied with sadomasochistic fantasies. The result can be destructive behavior or violence, and guilt over these impulses. Kilborne described these cycles as “shame-rage spirals” and “shame-guilt spirals.” Cycles of humiliation and fixation on one's own injury tend to restrict the capacity for symbol formation or “mentalization” and can contribute to destructive acting out. The panel examined how our resources as analysts can help us understand and ameliorate the destructive consequences of shame and humiliation in various social contexts.

Carlo Strenger, a Swiss-born Israeli analyst, described how shame and humiliation have been transmitted from generation to generation among Ashkenazi and Sephardic Jews in Israel, and how that process has affected political and social processes, with complex and harmful consequences. The “Old Jew,” a part of Israel's collective memory, was the European Jew, the victim of the Holocaust. “Old Jews” were “taken like sheep to slaughter.” Strenger hypothesized that European immigrants to Israel, vowing never to experience this kind of humiliation again, were nonetheless unable to integrate this shameful self-image and instead repressed it. They became the psychological opposite, powerful “New Jews,” who were tough, fearless, and able to fight to defend themselves.

Non-European Sephardic Jews began to arrive in Israel in the 1950s, bringing with them a distinct culture and a traditional lifestyle.

Panel held at the Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 24, 2004.

Panelists: Benjamin Kilborne (chair), Carlo Strenger, Elisabeth Young-Bruehl, Donald Moss, Stuart Twemlow.

Writing About Patients

IV. Patients' Reactions to Reading About Themselves

Judy Kantrowitz

Judy Kantrowitz

334 Kent Street
Brookline, MA 02446
Fax: 617-277-9564
E-mail: judy_kantrowitz@hms.harvard.edu

Judy Kantrowitz   Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Institute; AssociateProfessor of Psychology, Department of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School.

When analysands read about themselves in reports, their reactions range from anger, disappointment, or condemnation to a sense of appreciation or even idealization of the analyst. The eleven interviews reported here reflect only conscious responses; the unconscious layers were not probed for. It should be kept in mind also that the analysts of these patients might report very different stories. Other limitations are the small sample size and the representation only of patients who volunteered. Nonetheless, the information they provide may help analysts consider how and when writing about patients may influence their representation of themselves, the analyst, and analysis itself.

Writing About Patients

V. Analysts Reading about Themselves as Patients

Judy Kantrowitz

Judy Kantrowitz

334 Kent Street
Brookline, MA 02446
Fax: 617-277-9564
E-mail: judy_kantrowitz@hms.harvard.edu

Judy Kantrowitz   Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Institute; AssociateProfessor of Psychology, Department of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School.

The narratives of twenty analysts written about when they were patients are presented. Their stories provide suggestions about practices to avoid when writing clinical material, but no generalized prescriptions emerge. Individualization and sensitivity to the situation for each pair remain the best guide. The experiences these analysts recount run the gamut of emotions, from negative through neutral to positive. The neutral responses came mainly from twelve analysts who in the course of an interview about their own writing told of having been written about as patients. The other eight, volunteers who initiated contact for the sole purpose of reporting their experience of having been written about, appear on average to be motivated by stronger affective reactions. The era in which the analyst wrote also seems to have influenced the reactions; in earlier times, not asking permission was accepted professional practice. Today, however, it is increasingly common to ask permission when extended clinical examples are published. One problem specific to analyst-patients was concern about the loss of their role as patient when their analyst engaged them collaboratively in the writing.

responseSet.title: Cordelia, Lear, and Forgiveness
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Cordelia, Lear, and Forgiveness

Roy Schafer

Roy Schafer

390 West End Avenue #6F
New York, NY 10024
Fax: 212-877-4099
E-mail: royschafer@mindspring.com

Roy Schafer   Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

Painful human interactions are often followed by urges to forgive, be forgiving, or seek forgiveness. The insight analysands develop into their transferences highlights their finding gratification in constantly reenacting painful interactions. Their new understanding can make forgiveness seem irrelevant; waiving the question of forgiveness might then seem the wiser course to follow. Also thrown into question is whether total forgiveness of self and others can ever be achieved. Shakespeare's The Tragedy of King Lear raises these questions. There we encounter, first, the painful interaction of Cordelia and Lear and, finally, Cordelia's response, “No cause, no cause,” to a dying Lear's begging her forgiveness for having initially treated her cruelly. Cordelia's response seems to be waiving the question of guilt and forgiveness, but could it be wholehearted? In a search for answers, a reading of Cordelia's and Lear's lines is interwoven with interpretations of unconscious conflict that might be considered were one to encounter clinically a “Cordelia” abused by an aging and failing father at a turning point in her womanly development. Unconsciously, it is concluded, unforgivingness persists alongside the loving, insightful waiving of forgiveness made possible by higher-level ego functioning. Methodological reflections on reading and interpretation are included.

Rethinking Desire

The Objet Petit a in Lacanian Theory

Lewis Kirshner

Lewis Kirshner

306 Harvard Street
Cambridge, MA 02139
E-mail: lewis_kirshner@hms.harvard.edu

Lewis Kirshner   Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Institute; faculty, Massachusetts Institute of Psychoanalysis; Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School at Harvard South Shore Training Program and Brockton VA Medical Center.

The concept of the objet petit a is central to Lacan's theory of desire, which arguably represents his major contribution to psychoanalysis. It is an expression of the lack inherent in human beings, whose incompleteness and early helplessness produce a quest for fulfillment beyond the satisfaction of biological needs. The objet petit a is a fantasy that functions as the cause of desire; as such, it determines whether desire will be expressed within the limits of the pleasure principle or “beyond,” in pursuit of an unlimited jouissance, an impossible and even deadly enjoyment. Parallels between the objet petit a and Winnicott's transitional object are explored and its functions illustrated through analysis of Pedro Almodovar's film Talk to Her. A clinical case is presented in which the question of desire seemed crucial.

[[RunningHead]]

When the Analyst Dies: Dealing with the Aftermath

Tove Traesdal

Tove Traesdal

Ogmunds vei 14
0488 Oslo
NORWAY
Fax: +47 22 15 80 91
E-mail: totrae@online.no

Tove Traesdal   Training and Supervising Analyst, Institute for Psychotherapy, Oslo, Norway.

The body of literature on the theme of illness and death among analysts is surprisingly small, considering the frequency with which patients experience such incidents during an analysis, and the amount of suffering and/or iatrogenic injury that may ensue. In the bulk of this literature patients’ experiences of losing an analyst are treated in a descriptive way, mostly through personal reports. To move clinical thinking about this topic somewhat further, the implications for the patient when the analysis is interrupted are explored; in particular, a distinction is made between the loss of the person of the analyst as an incident on the reality level that needs to be mourned as such, and losing the analysis, the unique form of dialogue implied by the principle of free association. Consequences of the loss of this dialogue are analyzed with regard to the challenges it poses for clinicians called on to deal with the aftermath. Preventive measures within the analytic work can soften the impact of potential loss, as can the contribution of analytic institutions to dealing with the ensuing problems.
